Really, with the creation of the BIS, the IMF, and the World Bank, that created the construct of transnational capital that sits above governments: - Governments were subordinate to transnational capital through bondholders. - Companies were subordinate through the equity capital markets. - People were subordinate through borrowing to create fiat currency through the banks.

What defined that was having the strongest currency backed by gold, so that others would destroy their currency and you could weaponize currency wars.

71 之后：石油美元、隐蔽战争与 Safari Club

In '71 we came off the gold standard because everyone wanted their gold instead of dollars. They wanted to exchange their dollars for gold. America defaulted on that promise.

From that moment we entered into needing to engineer a covert war to make sure that there was constant demand for US debt and US treasuries — and that was the creation of the petrodollar. That's why you needed the regime change in Saudi Arabia.

George Bush set up a covert operation called Safari Club. Safari Club groomed people like Osama bin Laden — that led to 9/11 and various other CIA assets that would create all the wars in the Middle East, that would then lead to the forever war model, and everything that we're resolving and reversing today.

The idea was: if you price oil in dollars, you then need to reinvest it in government debt. That government debt would fuel the military-industrial complex. By having the most stable currency, everyone would need to lend it to the US government — while they launch wars against your country. And then you would be forced to spend those dollars back in the American stock market. Then that would be tied loans that would lead to military bases being set up in your country — because you either get regime change, a dictator installed, or you negotiate and do what Saudi did: set up something like the petrodollar.

反转的时刻

This all functions as long as the dollar is going up in value. Once people stop having faith in US treasuries, and the banking system stops using US treasuries as collateral, then that weakens the dollar and reverses all the chain. People end up going into gold, or the central banks because they don't want to hold treasuries. You end up having wars that lead to reasons like confiscation of treasuries — like happened to Russia — and then the whole trend reverses.

This is normally engineered by the FIC because they control the MIC.

金本位脱钩 → 中国崛起 → 多极化

But what actually really created transnational capital dominance to the hyper-concentrated degree we have today — it was the coming off of the gold standard. That led to the financialization and securitization of every company. From that, the FIC was able to think of the world in terms of global rather than nationalistic — and so they built up China, built the world's largest manufacturing base, and through globalization built China's wealth. Which is actually national wealth, because China is actually controlled by the government — one of the only countries in the world that's controlled by the government.

So they can't penetrate China. And so as China grew, the order changed. Now the American military can't even produce a weapon without China. We end up in a changing order at that stage.

六、Graeber 的"军事–铸币–奴隶复合体"

Alex: I'm not sure if you've ever came across this book of David Graeber, Debt: The First 5,000 Years?

Simon: I haven't actually read that one yet. It's on my reading list.

Alex: I would highly recommend it. In one chapter, there's something that I think would really tie in to a lot of what you're talking about — what he called the "military-coinage-slavery complex."

He uses the example of Alexander the Great, who through his conquest would require I think half a ton of silver daily to pay his army. Funnily enough, the word soldier itself is derived from solidus, the gold coin used to pay the troops.

The idea: he funded the cost of conquering Persia, then the Persian mines forcing enslaved war captives to mine and extract those precious metals, to then mint those bullions into coins. Wherever those coins would be used by the soldiers, they would create economies locally. This was the self-reinforcing cycle.

Today you could make a parallel in a much more sophisticated way — about the dollar, and about what you just said about the debt slavery process of tying people (executives, entrepreneurs, countries on a nation-state level), and now through the digital sphere with stablecoins, which we haven't talked about yet but which would be the next iteration of a much more sophisticated global scale.

七、今天的 FIC：BIS、央行、ETF 帝国与主权基金

Alex: The FIC today — is it governed by BlackRock and the other Big Three? Or is it much more complex than this?

Simon: A lot more complex.

巴塞尔的"飞地"：BIS

The Western central banking system has its own jurisdiction in Davos. The BIS — depending on the GDP of your country, you become a larger shareholder in BIS through your central bank. So the largest shareholder in BIS is the Federal Reserve. But as you manage capital outflows, you can change the GDP of a country. America has gone from like 70% of global GDP to about 25%. So the Federal Reserve is becoming less and less powerful in the BIS system over time.

三种央行模型：国有化、私有化、混合

Then you have different types of systems: - China: member of BIS, but it is a nationalized central bank that is owned by the government. There's state banking, mini banks, community banks — thousands of community banks in China, which is a fully nationalized banking system. The PBOC and the banking system is fully nationalized. - America: on the extreme, the Federal Reserve is owned by the banks, and the banks are private companies that are all public. Those public companies receive constant flows of capital via passive investments — mainly ETFs, pension funds, endowment funds, and foreign capital.

The owners of those banks as public companies is managed now by ETFs and asset managers, because now 50% of the stock market is ETF investments. The largest managers of ETFs are BlackRock, State Street, and Vanguard — collectively they manage approximately $25 trillion of assets. BlackRock about $14 trillion of assets.

主权财富基金的桌位

They then partner with countries that were able to maintain their own resources. A country like Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Norway, Singapore, and China — they all have the largest sovereign wealth funds in the world. They get a seat at the table co-investing with the FIC. But they represent their own — because Saudi is an absolute monarchy that owns its own resources. It's not private like the Federal Reserve system, it's not CCP nationalist like the Chinese system.

The FIC has partnered with sovereign wealth funds, and the central banking system is the mechanism for trying to get more resources and change those games. Every war is a constant state of fluid capital that's changing power.

Right now, Middle Eastern power is on the rise significantly as a result of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Even though it appears that it's declining, it's actually growing significantly as a result of this.

Saudi Aramco → BlackRock 的董事席

An example: with the rise of the petrodollar in Saudi Arabia, the partnership with China where China became the largest purchaser, America going to an energy exporter — that completely changed the petrodollar dynamics. Saudi got more power. What did they do? They floated through the investment bank part of Saudi Aramco — 1 to 2% of Saudi oil production. That gave them leverage with the investment banks in order to get a board seat on BlackRock. The CEO of Saudi Aramco became a board member on BlackRock. So now he can represent Saudi interest. So now the company BlackRock — that has 20,000 board seats on every major company — can manage a portfolio of where the war is, when the war goes or not.

Through managing technology like Aladdin — their AI technology that sovereign wealth funds use, treasury departments use, central banks use, pension funds use. You can scenario-plan with BlackRock's technology: if the Strait of Hormuz is going to close, and Iran retaliates, and these central banks start buying gold — what's the ideal portfolio and capital allocation?

Capital is plugged into the same system that then coordinates via ETFs and capital investments. Those flows is where you try and get leverage. How do I get more power over capital flows? That's the game. That's a constant state of tension.

People want an easy conspiracy theory of 300 people sat in a room in an organized cabal. To a certain degree, there are powerful people, but that changes all the time. Almost every government is subordinate to that capital structure. People think that they control the system by focusing on the government — when in reality the government and the politicians are owned by lobbies, and those lobbies are different powers within the capital structure (finance, military, technical, big pharma lobby interests).

People are just distracted constantly thinking: change a politician, change the game. No — they work for the same power structures that have funded both sides, all sides.

八、案例研究：英国的"政权更迭"流水线

Alex: If we now look at the situation more precisely in the UK with Keir Starmer resigning a few days ago — a lot of things happened that week, almost on a daily basis. How would you say that this changed anything?

Simon: Okay, let's pull over some of those concepts and bring them into a particular case study — the UK at the moment.

公理一：没有民主，只有被俘获的代议政体

Firstly, there's no such thing as a democracy. Democracies are captured. They are politicians that say they work for the people when they work for the lobbies. The lobby system is a pay-to-play system: you auction off tasks to politicians, and if they fulfill lobby tasks, then they get more money. When they get more money, they become more compromised. As they become more compromised, they rise to power more.

Starmer 与 Assange

Keir Starmer was a node for the military-industrial complex. He rose to fame as a lawyer, and as a lawyer he worked with military and deep state actors to try and take down Julian Assange. Julian Assange was creating a portal where they could leak government documents — and those government documents exposed the military-industrial complex for the million civilians that they killed in Iraq, all the children they murdered, all the crimes against humanity that they committed. Julian Assange allowed insiders to whistleblow and share what was really happening in these wars. That's a problem.

Starmer was part of an operation to claim that Julian Assange was a serial rapist, and then they tried to take him out and put him in prison so that they could get the keys that would then take down WikiLeaks. He proved his value to military contractors in that process. It was only recently that Julian Assange managed to get free, but by that time he had already risen to power.

媒体即叙事工厂

It's best to think of governments the way people describe Iran — regimes. These are regimes. They're not governments. They're not democracies. The media is fully captured as well. The media is there to perpetuate the narrative that the power that pays for all the advertising needs to be portrayed. They're owned by the FIC as well.

Media is a mechanism for projecting power, for manipulating people into an alternative reality. We all experienced that in COVID. We were watching the TV and every day we were being paralyzed into not leaving our house, because we were being told that we would be a grandma killer if we left our house, didn't wear a mask, didn't do social distancing. Every day we had a clock saying how many people we were killing, and it is our fault unless we get a vaccine.

That was the highest degree of media manipulation. We were shown deaths all around the world. That was to traumatize the people into compliance with an operation that was funded by the CIA — even Tulsi Gabbard said this was funded via leaks to Fauci, and Fauci was funding the lab in Wuhan and building bioweapons.

"死亡计数器"突然停了的那一天

One day, just one day, the death counter stopped. I remember the day incredibly well. Suddenly social distancing didn't matter. There were protests around the world, and suddenly protests were okay. We were told, "Oh, by the way, yes, everyone's vaccinated, COVID's not a problem anymore."

That day was the day that Russia invaded Ukraine. All of a sudden the media machine switched. No death counter, no social distancing, no problem with COVID, nothing. Suddenly we were all told that we need to defend democracy, and we are the defenders of democracy against the next Hitler, which was Putin.

The media machine comes into full tilt, and there's regime changes that happen.

英国十年内的七次"政权更迭"

If you look at the UK we had Boris Johnson, Rishi Sunak, then Keir Starmer. We've had seven different regime changes over the last 10 years in the UK. I don't live in UK anymore — I left UK. Each one of them served a purpose: - Boris Johnson was your lockdown guy — you can follow the money and see the allegiances that he was tied to in terms of big pharma. - Rishi Sunak was one of the largest investors via his family trust in vaccines and Moderna — the company that benefited from the vaccines. He was an ex-Goldman Sachs guy.

When you need to regime change, you basically take their compromise and you expose it. They're all blackmailed. That's how they come into power. That's how they were selected, not elected. And then the media machine makes you think that you voted them in. Every time they don't serve power to the next agenda, they get regime-changed — so that people know your regime change happens if you don't serve power.

  • Starmer was a node of the military-industrial complex. His job was to make sure that we believed we need to send all our money — while the country is being destroyed. Homelessness is at record highs. Suicide rates are at record highs. Drug addictions are at record highs. Inflation and wealth inequality are incomparable in terms of recent matters. But we need to send all our money to defend democracy in Ukraine. So billions is printed.
  • We had Liz Truss regime-changed by the Bank of England in between. That destroyed the bond market and the bondholders put in Starmer. We just had another one of those.

Starmer's job was to play into the Ukrainian narrative, which is a money-laundering mechanism for printing money at the Bank of England, printing money at the European Central Bank, allocating it across to these war zones in Ukraine, and then recycling up to prop up military-industrial complex sells and stock market valuations. There's corruption in between that pays off people. They use the media in order to paint Putin as Hitler for as long as they can.

What did Putin do? Putin arrested the oligarchs that wanted to privatize all the resources of Russia. He arrested them and nationalized the energy. Therefore he needed to become the next Hitler in the eyes of the West through the media.

They'll fight to the last death of the last Ukrainian. Eventually Ukraine will be given to Russia and BlackRock — and who was the number one funder through IMF collateralized loans? The Bank of England. So the Bank of England ends up with the land, the resources — as will the IMF, as will BlackRock, and as will Russia. They just carve it up, negotiate the rare earth minerals into the technical industrial complex. As long as you believe the media narrative that you're fighting for democracy, they'll keep going until the last Ukrainian is dead.

九、从 MIC → FIC → TIC：技术工业复合体登场

Alex: And so now, like, if we look at then the EU as a construct —

Simon: Sorry, I forgot. We just had the regime change today.

战争 → 重建 → 人口变动 → 战略紧张的循环商业模式

This is a business model of war, rebuild, demographic change, mix up, shake up, and monetize strategic tensions. Remember — this is why you fund communism, capitalism, fascism, have a Cold War, profit from war.

十二、Bitcoin 的位置：在控制网格之外，自我托管为王

Now that ties nicely into Bitcoin. I know that people are very mixed around Bitcoin. The reason is because it's digital — and because it's digital, it plays into those control grids. Those that love that realize you need to exit the system.

If you want to hold an asset, you've only got a few ways of doing it:

选项一：现金

What's the problem with cash? It gets devalued over time. So you lose your purchasing power. But it's nice for short-term expenses — if you happen to not lose all your value in the time before you spend it.

选项二：黄金

Gold has preserved its value for 5,000 years. Its supply only increases at an average of 1.5% to 2% historically. But most people when they need to store it at scale end up giving it to BlackRock, and they end up with paper gold instead. Or they end up with a tokenized version of gold, which is "you will own nothing and we will hold the gold for you."

Alex: Or you buy physical gold at GoldRepublic and we store it for you on your behalf.

Simon: Correct. So then you need to come up with a mechanism for storing your own gold — or you come up with the best type of custodian where you think they'll give you your gold under the set of legal protection. But it's still difficult, and most people end up storing their gold with someone else under the best set of circumstances where they think they may be able to get that gold in case they need it.

Or you have a safe in your house, you own your own gold, and you're autonomous. The downside of that is that at scale it's a big risk, and most people can't do it. And then you don't have to pay the fees to store the gold.

选项三：Bitcoin —— 数字主权

So Bitcoin then basically said: you can own your own value, but it's digital. You can send it to whoever you want as long as you self-custody it. The monetary policy is enforced by a decentralized network of miners and nodes that can never change it. There'll only ever be 21 million Bitcoin. People lose it over time when they lose access to the keys. Effectively 20 million bitcoins have already been mined. There's only another million to be mined. You can plan out the monetary policy any time into the future and know that more and more people will probably lose those Bitcoin because they lost the key, or died, or didn't plan how to pass it on. So it is a fixed supply.

比特币的拓扑：白皮书 → 矿工 → 节点 → 自托管

  • Open-source code: an anonymous developer released a white paper called Satoshi Nakamoto, and a bunch of developers came around and started being involved in coding it. The early developers were people like Hal Finney, Gavin Andresen and various others.
  • Mining is geographically distributed: Iran is the largest sovereign Bitcoin miner with nuclear energy mining Bitcoin. UAE is another big Bitcoin miner. Russia is. America has about 20 different public companies that collectively manage 40% of the hash rate. China has 21% of the hash rate. The network can never be taken down. It is now the largest distributed supercomputer that exists in the world today.
  • Nodes: the rules are enforced by anybody that wants to run a node — and it's cheap to run a node. As long as you self-custody your Bitcoin, you can connect it to a node, verify your own transactions, store the entire history, and contribute to enforcing the rules whenever any of the coders try to change it, or the miners want to signal a new change. There's 25,000 of those nodes around the world run by anyone that wants to run one. 25,000 sovereign people that can control their own value, send their own value, and know that the monetary policy will never change.

But it is digital. It does rely upon energy. So there are choke points.

十三、Satoshi 之谜：Len Sassaman 与 David Chaum 的荷兰渊源

People look at choke points and say, "Okay, so Satoshi Nakamoto, this anonymous person created Bitcoin. Why?" You can theorize. I've got my own theory of why.

If you go back to that time, there was a movement to try and create digital currencies from the cypherpunk movement. One of those attempts was DigiCash. DigiCash was created by a guy called David Chaum. David Chaum was in Netherlands teaching at a university two other cypherpunk cryptographers. One was called Len Sassaman.

Len Sassaman was assassinated. What? Sorry, let me correct that. He killed himself and allegedly committed suicide.

Alex: He got "suicided."

Simon: Well — again, my belief. I want to be respectful of the dads and his family. He allegedly committed suicide one month after the developer that Satoshi Nakamoto handed the project over to — Gavin Andresen — met with the CIA. One month after, Len Sassaman lost his life. And so did Hal Finney, the first one to ever mine a Bitcoin and contribute to that.

Gavin Andresen is still alive today, but he went off into another project. He forked off Bitcoin and tried to create an alternative version of Bitcoin, and left the Bitcoin community.

The point is: I personally believe that Satoshi Nakamoto was Len Sassaman. There's significant evidence to that effect. And I believe that it was created from his mentorship by David Chaum in Netherlands, and Hal Finney was the one that most likely was involved in the coding while Len Sassaman probably released the white paper. Len Sassaman and Hal Finney are no longer with us today.

一个反直觉的假设：Bitcoin 是稳定币/CBDC 项目的"叛逃产物"

What was David Chaum doing? David Chaum and DigiCash was shut down because they tried to create private transactions on top of the banking system — and because the banks could shut off the choke point, they were able to disappear that project.

I personally believe that they set about iterating through central intelligence stablecoins and central bank digital currencies as digital currencies — and I believe that Len Sassaman may have been involved in that project, and he released the white paper as a breakaway movement from those digital currencies and digital fiat currencies that are now stablecoins and CBDCs. He released the white paper as a rogue agent from that project — because otherwise, I don't think it was meant to be open-source code on a decentralized network with node operators where anyone can run a node.

That's the antithesis to the digital currencies that are currently being confiscated — like Iran stablecoins. You can call Tether and have them frozen, and Scott Bessent did it. So I believe it was a breakaway movement, with many many infiltration attempts to try and bring the project closer to centralization. Because I was involved in 2011 when Bitcoin was $3, and got to meet many of those developers, and was invited to many of the early squats where Bitcoin coders were working on the code, I've seen many many of the infiltration attempts by central intelligence to try and infiltrate the network.

Still those exist to this day. But Bitcoin, I believe, has maintained and preserved its resistance against those. It still is open-source code with a distributed supercomputer, the largest in the world. The rules are enforced by anyone that wants to run a node. Anyone can own their own value still today in self-custody, send it to anyone they want without a government, without a bank. The money supply, I still believe, will never ever change.

十四、隐私问题：CoinJoin、Lightning 与"主权 vs 屈从"光谱

Alex: What about the privacy though? Because Bitcoin is pseudo-anonymous. People hold Bitcoin — I hold Bitcoin, Bitcoin is the reason why I got into finance — but apart from holding it and all the features that you just described, there's for now not much more I can do because it's locked either in the wallet or, in some cases, an exchange (which is not recommended). It doesn't really move, and it can be traced through KYC/AML procedures.

What is your conclusion to that? Is there maybe another layer on top of Bitcoin that will solve this, or is this not a problem to you at all?

Simon: The on-ramps and off-ramps mean that whenever you engage in fiat currency, you have to play fiat games — which is KYC, AML and all that. But I can send my Bitcoin to anyone I want to, and no one can stop that.

Now, law enforcement exists. I still need to play within the confines of the law. If I break the law, I break the law, and I need to face all the consequences of that. If somebody wants to steal my physical value, they can try and steal it.

CoinJoin 与 Lightning

Part of the benefits of self-custody is that there's a couple of other features. You can run a node. You can also use a CoinJoin — and that's completely legal. One of the first bits of technology that the developers worked on was CoinJoins — combining transactions with millions of other transactions so that you can have private transactions.

The next was to set up Layer 2 — where you can take the value that you want to spend rather than save, and you can transact in a Layer 2 network like Lightning. This is the path that the open-source community has taken us down. If you want to obfuscate your transactions in CoinJoins — as long as it's legal in your country — you can do that. At the moment it is completely legal to do that.

It doesn't circumvent the law. It allows you to control your rails within the legal system that you decide to live in.

主权 vs 屈从光谱

Remember: I often talk about there is complete subordination on one side of a spectrum, and there is complete sovereignty on the other side of a spectrum. My life has been going from complete subordination to as much sovereignty as I can get.

I still live on an island which still has a government, and we have a community, and I need to play within the rules of that community of social behavior and social consensus. I need to pay the tax and my contribution in the community that I live in. If I don't play within the confines of the rules of this country, I have to accept the full consequences of the jurisdiction and the legal system by which I live in.

I'm still subordinate to the social contract with my wife. I still may have a business that has shareholders. I still may have some money in fiat currency, which means that part of my wealth is subordinate to the bank — and I fully expect them to give me all sorts of problems every time I try and spend it at scale.

These are all subordination vehicles. My and your job is to wake up every week and say: if I take that action, am I more subordinate or more sovereign? And who am I subordinate to, and how do I move towards sovereign in that aspect of my life? That's the job.

But nobody is fully sovereign. If you believe in God, then you are subordinate to the rules at which God governs the world. If you are atheist, you are still subordinate to those structures of what you think the rules are of the world. We are all subordinate to something or someone — but some are completely subordinate debt slaves.

奴隶制的演化

That's what the FIC wanted to create. The concept of debt was originally a mechanism for getting somebody using their labor to repay the debt — so you actually could use them as slaves and they could work off their debt.

It wasn't what we ended up creating as slavery in the West, where we actually took Africans from their island, destroyed their currency, shipped them in chains over to here and say "you are mine." Originally, slavery was the concept of: you took out a debt and you couldn't repay that debt, so you were able to work off that debt through your labor and your body. Originally, it led to industries like prostitution and all sorts of stuff because women used their bodies in order to pay off debts. But it was working towards a debt of becoming free from your slave master.

It was only the Europeans and the Americans that created a version of slavery where you literally just take people in chains and say, "You're mine, with no possibility to work it off — you're owned by me."

They replaced that when slavery became illegal in most countries. They replaced it with debt subordination — make you a collateralized debt obligation where you earn just enough to pay off the interest, and you work your whole life just to pay the interest. As you progress, you take on more debt and pay more interest, and you do that forever. That's the slavery that they created to replace the European and American version of slavery that they did to the Africans.

十六、Wall Street 的 Bitcoin：Saylor 与 Strategy 的真正角色

Alex: So this is sovereign Bitcoin. And then there's Wall Street Bitcoin — which is Michael Saylor, treasury companies — and maybe perhaps a bit of context about also how the FIC then is trying to rein in this sovereign Bitcoin that we just described. How would you paint this picture?

Simon: Okay. The FIC doesn't mind two types of people, but they don't want the third type.

他们不介意的人

Type 1: Broke people in debt with freedom of speech. They don't care. You can yap away. You can talk about Epstein. You can say whatever you want. Some countries you can't, others you can. You can complain about everything. You can say that Trump's a pedophile in Epstein's network — whatever you want to say. They don't mind, as long as you're broke and in debt and keep yapping away. Type 2: Wealthy people that are captured by the system. Elon Musk is an extreme example — people where they've got collateralized wealth in DTCC-owned custody structures.

Alex: So what is DTC?

Simon: Basically you don't own your shares. If you own a share, you own a claim from the broker that you own that share. That broker has a claim against DTCC, which is a central clearing which is owned by the brokers. So it's all promise that you own that share. In the bankruptcy process, in the breaking of the system, DTCC owns the share. The broker owns a claim against DTCC, and you own a claim against the broker. So you're layers removed from your share — but you don't own them. You just log in and it looks like you own it.

Same with gold in custody. You log in and it looks like you own it — but what legal ways do you have to get it? That's the contract. Same with Bitcoin in custody, any of these custodians.

They don't mind you having all your wealth in custody — using it as collateral, leveraged, with lots of equity that they control and debt — and very powerful, and very high net worth, so that they manage your net worth for you. They don't mind as many of those as possible. They'll capture as many of them — where you're on the same team either through shared interest, leverage, or blackmail, whatever they need to do depending on how powerful you are and what game you're playing in.

他们不要的"第三种人"

What they don't want is people that are free to speak with wealth outside the system — that are willing to speak against the system, reallocate capital, and actually drive people to taking capital either away from here or galvanizing these people. Those are the people that they don't want.

Wherever there were the biggest uprisings in those came from: the gold community, the cash community, and the Bitcoin community. So they set about: - Eliminating the cash community by making everything digital banking, - Centralizing all the gold through central banking, - And now they want to do the same to Bitcoin.

Operation Chokepoint 2.0 与对 Bitcoin 的"产品化收编"

They created an operation called Operation Chokepoint 2.0, where they created companies that you could destroy to remove credibility, manufacture a crisis, take out all the banks that were servicing the crypto companies — and replace them with Wall Street.

So then you got the birth of the FIC players like Goldman Sachs, JP Morgan creating options markets, derivatives markets, perpetual markets. Silicon Valley invested in all the companies.
