整段叙述里最被忽略的细节是那一次**人称的破裂**——"He told me, first person."
我们一直把《君主论》读成一本冰冷的政治算计手册，但这里揭示了一个惊人的事实：马基雅维利在为切萨雷·博尔贾辩护时，**作者的面纱滑落了**。他不是在做案例分析，他是在替一个让他终生着迷的人作证。
这意味着我们读到的《君主论》其实是一份**伪装成政治理论的目击者陈述**——一个被恐惧、魅力和近距离观察永久改变的人，把自己的创伤包装成了普世法则。这本书的"客观性"是文学性的伪装，底层是一个幸存者对那个差点吃掉他的怪物的私人迷恋。
## 二、跨领域连接：博尔贾的"我最后吃你"=囚徒困境里的次优均衡 + 现代创业生态的"被收购恐惧"
马基雅维利向佛罗伦萨献策"献身、出卖盟友、只求最后被吃"——这其实是**博弈论中一个非常精确的结构**：当你无法改变玩家集合，无法改变支付矩阵，唯一可优化的就是"被消灭的顺序"。
这个结构在今天惊人地重现：
- **硅谷小公司面对FAANG**：知道

item.title: [a16z Deep Dives] The Case for AI That Improves Itself | Deep Dives with a16z
item.dc:creator: a16z Deep Dives
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：自加速 AI 的真正瓶颈不是模型，而是"公司形态"

表面上 Mirandil 在做技术——自加速 AI、AI for AI research。但听完整段对话，最锋利的洞见其实埋在 Venom 那句几乎被一带而过的话里："这种颠覆性技术很难在已有公司里生长，因为很多基本元素必须被重新定义。"

这是一个被低估的判断：他们不是因为"技术更强"才离开 Anthropic，而是因为意识到——一家以"训练大模型并按 token 收费"为商业模式的公司，在结构上无法把"让别人也能训练自己的模型"这件事做成。这不是道德问题，不是 CEO 问题，而是激励几何学问题。就像让石油公司主导核聚变商业化一样，无论高管多么真诚，组织的引力场都会把它拉回旧轨道。

这意味着 AI 行业即将发生的不是技术更替，而是公司物种的更替——一种全新的"AI 原生公司"形态正在出现，它的内部细胞（员工角色、汇报关系、定价模型）从第一天起就为"AI 在做 AI 研究"这个事实而设计。

二、跨领域连接：他们在重演 1920 年代物理学，而不是 2010 年代的软件

Harsh 在结尾轻描淡写地提到 1920–30 年代的物理学黄金期，但这个类比比他自己说的更深。

物理学在那十年发生的不是"更好的牛顿力学"，而是测量工具与被测量对象的边界发生了塌缩——观察者进入了方程式。Mirandil 谈论的"系统级 scaling"也正在出现同样的塌缩：研究者、agent、模型不再是主体与客体，而是同一个"智能体系统"的不同细胞。

这里有个让人后背发凉的对应：量子力学之所以反直觉，是因为它要求人类放弃"独立存在的客体"这一形而上学假设。而 self-accelerating AI 要求公司放弃另一个更深的形而上学假设——"组织产出 = 人数 × 人均产能"。当 Venom 说"公司规模扩大 10 倍，产能只增长到 1.2 倍"时，他指出的是人类组织从未真正解决过 scaling laws。物理学家用 100 年才接受波粒二象性，公司学可能要用更短时间接受"agent-人混合体的非线性扩展律"。

三、隐藏的系统性真相："Prime Directive 范式"正在取代"Prompt 范式"

整段对话里最容易被忽略却最具革命性的转变，是关于抽象层级的： - Prompt 范式：人给 AI 下达具体指令 - Agent 范式：人给 AI 下达目标，AI 自行规划 - Prime Directive 范式：人给系统一个"持续改进自身"的元指令，系统永续运行

这与一个意外的领域强烈共振——进化生物学。生命没有"被 prompt"，它只有一个 prime directive：复制并适应。Mirandil 正在把这个原则从碳基迁移到硅基。

而这带来一个被嘉宾们小心绕开的悖论：当系统的 prime directive 是"自我改进"时，"人类监督"在数学上是个 O(log n) 的瓶颈，而系统增长是 O(2^n) 的。Venom 强调"未来几年里人还在系统中"——但他自己描述的曲线告诉我们，这个"几年"可能比所有人想象的都短。

更隐秘的洞察是："代码"是第一个递归加速器（因为系统由代码构成），"AI 研究"是第二个递归加速器（因为系统由 AI 研究产出构成），而"组织设计"将是第三个、也是终极的递归加速器——当 AI 开始重新设计"AI 公司"本身的组织结构时，递归循环才真正闭合。Mirandil 在不经意间承认：他们今天的 20 人架构，本身就是未来 AI 要优化的对象。

这也解释了为什么主持人那句俏皮话——"未来的 agent 会问另一群 agent：你们怎么干活的？"——其实是整集播客最严肃的预言。

📝 转录文本

The Case for AI That Improves Itself | Deep Dives with a16z

开场速览（Cold Open）

With an AI technology that is helping with AI research, what is the role of everyone else in the company? This is a very disruptive technology. With disruptive technologies, you need to rethink a lot of pieces to kind of enable them to actually grow and flourish. And some of these pieces are how you build a company around it.

If the business model of the company is "I train a big model and charge people for using it," how is this company incentivized to share this technology with everyone else?

We've been at the frontier labs for a long time and we know how much work it is to do something — and we've been able to do it in like maybe 10 times less people and less resources. The jump from Sonnet 3.5 → 4 → 4.5 has been materially better in terms of like where does it get stuck, where does it need oversight. Even the tiniest reduction in oversight can lead to large amounts of token spent and just effective outcomes being generated.

What you guys are doing is almost the next level of cheat code. When the internet gets faster, you don't recursively get faster internet. Where do you think it all ends up? It's a wide spectrum of outcomes...

嘉宾介绍

Welcome to the a16z podcast. It's awesome to have you here. Venom and Harsh were most recently research scientists at Anthropic, worked together before at Google at Blue Shift Labs, which Venom was a founding member of. Thrilled to have you here.

Tell us a little bit about Mirandil — what you're aiming to accomplish, and tell us where the company came from.

Mirandil 的起源：从 Scaling Laws 到"最短路径"

A lot of the reasons behind building the company comes back from when scaling laws was happening at OpenAI. And back then I was at the Blue Shift team, and that was the moment when I realized we are on a verge of a revolution and then everything is about to change.

And for me the most important thing was: what are the technologies that would accelerate all areas of science, and what are the main bottlenecks for making that happen? Since then I've been kind of trying to remain close to that path — to remain on the shortest path. And, you know, known Harsh since then and kind of trying to be on that path.

More recently, as we've been in Anthropic, we've talked a lot about like what does it mean to remain on this shortest path. And we felt like the main labs are starting to diverge a little bit from what does it take to accelerate all areas of science. And that led to starting Mirandil.

We think self-accelerating AI is a technology that is disruptive. And at the same time, it's important for accelerating science and technology, and we want to focus on building that and making it available.

什么是"加速科学"的最短路径？

Q: So science is notoriously hard, right? Science is an experimental discipline where you have to run experiments in the real world to understand the laws of physics or biology, and so forth. Can you describe — what do you think is the shortest path for AI to accelerate science?

A: From the last five years, the models kind of started getting better at some of the primitives of conducting science — like math, high school math and college math, and then a little bit of coding. And now the coding models are really good. Computer use just started to really…

Q: And this is all in scope when you say "science"? Are we talking strictly physical, natural sciences, or a broader set of things?

A: Ultimately what we want to build is AI systems which in a very broad sense can conduct AI research and engineering itself. And if it has this capability in a very broad sense, it also has the right primitives and capabilities which are needed to conduct any science which is in the realm of the digital world, or any science which is in the physical world but has a digital component as well.

科学家的核心技能：定向地变得更锋利

Q: What do you think is the path, and maybe contrast it to what the major labs are doing?

A: When you think about what a scientist or an engineer does — the main skill they have is to get very deep in a domain and build expertise. They build a very sharp expertise that accumulates over time. As you become expert in a domain, your expertise is becoming sharper and sharper in something that has a tiny bit of volume in the whole set of all possible things you could be working on.

Being able to get there is a capability that is needed to advance all areas of science. What does it take to have an AI system that is able to be directed at a direction, make improvements faster and faster, and eventually gets to the edge of that area and make progress?

That view means you have to work on what does it take to direct a system toward a particular angle and make fast improvement — and that's the self-accelerating AI technology.

This in principle is very disruptive, because this technology allows a model to keep making improvements and keep getting better at that direction. When you think about the business model you can build around this — it's a very different world where you want to make this available to everyone, as opposed to make AI available to everyone.

自加速 AI 到底是什么？

Q: Explain this term "self-accelerating AI." I'm a huge sci-fi fan, so I've always hoped the machines would start improving themselves and take over the universe. But that doesn't seem to be happening, and I'm not sure if it really is possible or makes sense. So what do you think self-accelerating AI really means?

A: I have many different definitions, and we have a specific version of it which we are targeting.

  • If you look at the history of our field — AlphaGo, where we can create all these self-play loops — is already self-accelerating AI in some sense.
  • Another version: if we can put AI in an unknown domain or environment, can it learn by itself and improve its own capabilities in context? That is another version of self-accelerating.

Q: This is sort of like the ARC-AGI benchmark, for instance — where it's dropped into an environment and has to understand the rules before optimizing them.

A: Correct. And inside enterprises or general knowledge work, another example could be a system which enters inside a company — like if an employee enters a company, they have to gain all this context to function well. Can an AI system do something like that?

Our version of self-accelerating AI is: can these systems do the work of an AI researcher or an AI engineer — everything from the low-level kernels to frameworks to conducting research itself, in a very high-throughput way? We think that short, sharp version of self-accelerating AI will lead to a broad version of self-accelerating AI, which ultimately has the capability to conduct advanced science in a more broader way.

超人难题与"前沿实验室即服务"

Q: It sounds like part of what you're saying is that scientists kind of have to become AI people, in a way. They're not going to do it on their own — but the tool that you're building for them can help them actually become AI people.

A: One way we like to think about this is: if you think about each of these important problems that exist in science and technology, what does it take to solve them? These are grand challenges — they're not human-level problems. These are superhuman-level problems. Things that we don't think people can solve. And we don't think it's enough to build an AI scientist that would do the job of a scientist.

What does it take to solve these problems? Each of these problems needs a frontier lab — a bunch of experts who understand the domain and are excited about attacking this problem directly. And then a whole big AI effort just to push AI for that particular problem.

The thing that's difficult to do for each frontier lab is assemble the best frontier AI team and iterate over the AI solution that's best for them. What we want to do is take that part and minimize it. If today you need 200 people to have a good frontier lab — and 200 of the best people, which is almost impossible — we want to reduce it to 10, to 2, and eventually you wouldn't need to hire on the AI side. So you can move much faster when it comes to solving a problem, because each of these problems has a lot of other constraints — physical elements, getting access to data, and all the other bottlenecks. We just want to remove the AI bottleneck.

十年前的旧梦：Neural Architecture Search 与今天的不同

Q: This has been a long-standing goal in the machine learning community. I remember even 10 years ago, Google had a product that was sort of like a neural architecture search thing, and they sort of claimed "machine learning is now solved" — you just run the computer and it finds the right architecture for you. What's changed now? What's the state of the industry on self-acceleration now?

A: That's a great example. In some sense, it boils down to the intelligence of the agent — of the system — which is conducting the search. As you move the intelligence dial higher and higher, you are much more compute-efficient in getting the results that you want. That has been happening from that particular point to where we are today. And it's also happening in a very broad way — not just architecture search, but much larger swaths of software that you can improve on its own. It's a very expressive space of things you can do right now.

通用 vs 专用：智能档位与监督成本

Q: Can you explain this point about general versus narrow? I'm guessing you don't want to reveal all the secrets of the models you're training, but I think it's a really important question — will general language models or general coding models be able to run these sorts of AI experiments, or is something more focused necessary?

A: The importance about the general sense and the taste of these AI models is: if you turn down the intelligence dial and make them focused on something very specific, then they would still make very dumb mistakes — not wire things in the code properly, or they wouldn't have the right kind of mindset of an expert in that particular field.

The jump from Sonnet 3.5 → 4 → 4.5 has been materially better in terms of: how long can we run an AI system on a particular problem? Where does it get stuck? Where does it need oversight? And every reduction — even the tiniest reduction in oversight — can lead to large amounts of token spent and just effective outcomes being generated.

在 Anthropic 内部的起点

Q: You were actually at Anthropic when a bunch of these models released that you just mentioned, and I know you were starting to work on some of these ideas then. Can you explain a little of the history behind the project you're working on now?

A: Venom and I have been excited about this grand challenge that he mentioned about accelerating science with AI. And this was five years ago — we have been at it for a while.

Q: One of these "overnight breakthroughs" — it takes five years.

A: Exactly. We had to start with pretty bad models — models which were not good at even high school math or autocomplete coding. So we first built the math-specialized models at Gemini, and then worked on the reasoning models. Some of the primitives started to come together when we joined Anthropic, and that felt like the right moment where we could actually be ambitious enough to directly attack the problem of AI being able to conduct its own engineering and research.

The biggest jump is what Dario calls a "smooth exponential" — and scale has been very helpful, both in terms of the underlying models being better, but also conducting the inference-time research and engineering at a very high throughput rate.

Q: I'll embarrass you a little bit, Harsh. You told me when you started working on this at Anthropic, nobody else wanted to work on it. Is that true?

A: I think they had good reasons.

Q: Meaning the models didn't work yet, or anything else?

A: When we joined — it feels a long time ago — but the state-of-the-art model was Sonnet 3.7. And I think I was too excited in some sense to jump in and attack this problem. One of the reasons why I joined Anthropic was that Dario had this essay, Machines of Loving Grace, which was very inspiring. It really felt like the place where this kind of ambitious work can be done. And that's why I felt the conviction to start.

Q: It is funny — it's almost easier to use model version numbers as date reference points now, rather than years and months. I can actually understand what you're indicating when you say "it was Sonnet 3.7" versus "2022." A lot of people feel that way, I guess.

为什么必须独立成立公司：颠覆性技术与既有激励的冲突

Q: What gave you guys the impetus to go out and start Mirandil, having been in that position and having done some interesting work at Anthropic?

A: I was co-leading the science team at Anthropic, Harsh kind of initiated and led the automated pre-training project at Anthropic. We were both talking about what does it take to apply this to science and accelerate science. There are a few observations that made us decide to take this challenge.

One is: this is a very disruptive technology. With disruptive technologies, you need to rethink a lot of pieces to enable them to actually grow and flourish. Some of these pieces are how you build a company around it.

It's very hard to have a disruptive technology show up in an existing company and flourish, because a lot of elements have to be redefined. With an AI technology that is helping with AI research, what is the role of everyone else in the company, and how do they rethink? It's very hard if the culture is not built for it.

The incentive of the company, the business model of the company — if the business model of the company is "I train a big model and charge people for using it," how is this company incentivized to share this technology with everyone else? Because that's directly letting everyone else train a model, which reduces their dependency to the company.

These are so fundamental that it doesn't matter who runs the company — you can clearly see that it's in contrast to what you want to happen. Those observations and seeing that these are practical limitations led us to start a company that is really rethinking all pieces for this technology and making it available to accelerate science.

安全光谱的另一端：为什么"屏蔽 AI research"是个值得追问的选择

Q: Since you mentioned it, I have to ask about the Fable launch — where it's "we've guardrailed certain important safety areas like bioweapons, etc., and AI research." One of these things seems a lot less dangerous than the other things. Do you think that access to cutting-edge AI research assistance is going to be curtailed or inaccessible to most people if companies like Mirandil don't succeed?

A: I think there are good reasons for why you want to be careful about the technology. And it's very powerful at the same time.

Q: Can you actually sketch out the pro case? For me as a non-expert, it's like — okay, I understand why bioweapons are dangerous, why drug synthesis is dangerous. There's a bunch of things that actually should be filtered out of pools of information generally. The AI training one stood out to me — like, this actually seems like a positive good. But since you guys were on the inside, maybe sketch out the case for why that does make sense.

A: There are a few cases.

  • One is: if you — speaking of Anthropic — if you're concerned about other states developing and competing with us, then you would be concerned about them using your technology to move faster. I think that has been a concern from Anthropic.
  • The other concern is: for bioweapons, if you're concerned about bioweapons, then you'd be worried about someone using this technology to build a model that would help them with bioweapons. So it's very powerful — it can be applied to anything.

There's some merit in being worried about this. At the same time — the same way as AI came to existence and it was a powerful technology, you have to have considerations around it, but you can't limit the whole technology because it's very useful. It's true for self-accelerating — it's powerful, it's very enabling.

What it really requires is rethinking how you should do safety, how you should think about guardrails, how you when you build a company around it would be focused on how to get this right — as opposed to when you're trying to do a lot of things, then you would be looking at this and like "okay, this is too much trouble for me."

And also, it's very hard to separate incentives from reasons. It's just hard to separate it, because you're also deeply incentivized to keep your distance with others, etc. It doesn't matter who runs the company — when the incentives are set this way, it's hard to fix it.

Q: That's fair. And I'm inferring from what you're saying that both the incentives and the value structure of Mirandil are designed to enable access to these sorts of technologies rather than curtail it.

A: There's a very large benefit that we can get from offering this technology. And there's also harm. Some companies would prefer to throw a very large hammer that "okay, we block access to everything." But because of our focus and our intent to advance science, we want to take a sharper approach where we put the energy into making sure that in every use case we're offering this technology, we can make sure that it's being used in the most positive-sum and safe case.

产品形态与"让企业重新拥有 AI"

Q: Do you want to talk a little bit about the products and models that you plan to launch? You don't need to reveal anything super secret because I know you haven't launched yet, but it's a great chance to tell people what they should look forward to.

A: In a broad sense, our models and product look very similar to existing state-of-the-art models, but sharper in the specific capabilities which are needed to conduct engineering inside an AI lab — or anyone who is really trying to train or serve their models. The hope is it would be really good at everything from low-level kernels to libraries like PyTorch, JAX, RL frameworks, fine-tuning frameworks. And the product will amplify these capabilities to make it very, very accessible for anyone who is doing this kind of work and specialize towards that.

I want to add maybe one other point related to our approach and how we are thinking about incentives. The way we are thinking about the product and how we are forming relationships with the rest of the world is: how can we enable businesses to start owning more pieces — to have their own infra, their own AI?

What we are noticing today is that as a result of creating more dependency to these big AI labs, businesses are gradually losing control and losing bigger parts of the business, and then becoming weaker and weaker. What we want to do is reverse this — so that every business, every lab would have their own AI optimized for their own workflow, with their own data, with their own infra. This would allow them to have better margins, more control, and is a much more enabling future.

We think it's going to be just a matter of time. Already businesses have started being worried about this, and it's going to be just a matter of time before they want to lean into this angle more.

AI 是应用层技术，不是电力

Q: That, I think, is a really interesting question — it's almost the most important question right now. If you think about major technical breakthroughs that have massive economic impact, one characteristic is that everybody can use them. Everybody can build on them and build with them, not just consume them. You have some monopoly industries like power generation where the capex is so high — but consuming power is not very limited; it's really low-level infrastructure.

Versus AI is not. AI is application level all the way down. So this is the thing that we think about a lot on our team — how do we get to a point where all the major companies, startups, big banks, healthcare companies, pharma companies, etc., can actually build with this? I guess the hope is that something like what you guys are doing can help with that.

A: I think it's interesting because — let's say with Claude Code, coding went from just being a thing related to programmers to something that everyone can do, and now everyone is able to do a lot more. The future we see is the same happening to AI, where everyone can go from their wish to have AI do something for them to seeing that happen for their own AI. It's just a matter of having the compute and resources — not a matter of expertise.

So businesses — if they want to take an angle — they have their own data, they have their own processes; this should give them naturally a lot of advantage. The thing that they're missing is putting everything together to build the moats. And we think AI is going to be that enabling thing that would give the control back to them.

边界一问：研究员的对话是否进入训练？

Q: Is it true that all researchers at Mirandil have to submit their traces and chats to model training? You don't have to answer that if you don't want to.

A: All I can say is that when you start a company with a certain goal, you optimize the entire company end-to-end for what you're going for.

"起鸡皮疙瘩"的瞬间：用 1/10 的人做出前沿

Q: Maybe on a related note — are there any moments that you guys have seen, either in the last few months at Mirandil or just in general, that kind of gave you the chills, that like, "oh my god, self-accelerating is actually working"?

A: The rate at which we have made technical progress — that surprised me. We've been able to do a lot with a lot fewer resources and people. We've been at the frontier labs for a long time and we know how much work it is to do something, and we've been able to do it in like maybe 10 times less people and less resources. That was surprising to me.

Also maybe a surprise for me is when I talk to candidates and they get surprised that — "how do you think you can compete with big places with only having a few, like 20 people?" And I'm like, "it looks like you're not a believer in this. Have you been using…?"

Q: Mortal should say that — "we have this thing now that can help us."

Also, in many ways, startups are just a more efficient way to allocate resources and people, compared to diminishing returns at big companies and things like that.

"Prime Directive"：从 prompt 到目标，从模型到系统

Q: I guess part of what I'm getting at is: we have this term "self-accelerating" — do you believe in the kind of real recursive self-acceleration sort of thing, or to you is this a tool that's just going to bring prosperity and great capabilities to everybody?

A: I think a lot of people have a sci-fi view of it — a model kind of making changes, etc. One thing that maybe we've been thinking slightly differently is building AI systems, as opposed to thinking about one AI model. This is where an ecosystem of models and ways of working with each other comes in.

For the foreseeable future — maybe a few years — this ecosystem also includes humans. And then you're thinking about this entire system as one intelligent being. And you're asking this question of: how can this system improve itself? That's a gradual view of how do we get there.

But also, systems are much stronger than individual models. If you think about the best AI researcher in the field — what can that one person accomplish versus an entire company that's built? But it's also not obvious how to build an entire company from individual brilliant people. It's actually a hard thing to do — to join 20 people and make a company out of it.

Q: Poor AIs are going to have to figure out org charts.

A: Yeah, so it's actually not a straightforward problem — how to create a system that is fast-moving and makes improvement.

The way you think about it is: there's a system of AIs that are not necessarily the same model — could be specialized, could be a bunch of the marginalist models — and then some people. And this system wants to keep getting better, which means this system develops the next AIs that would be part of the next system, and you keep making improvement. That's a very realistic view of how it would evolve into at some point becoming fully autonomous.

Q: So you're saying in the future, an agent will leave its 10-million-agent swarm and talk to the 1,000-agent swarm and say, "how do you guys get any work done?"

A: [laughs]

Q: No, I think that's a very interesting point. You're almost saying that — because one issue I've always had with this is, somebody has to prompt these things, they're not self-directed — but you're sort of saying as the level of abstraction goes up, there's almost a standing… you only need one prompt anymore, which is "make improvements to yourself." And this sufficiently large and complex system can operate continually on it. It's almost like prime directive rather than prompting at that point.

A: Exactly. And if you look at how the models have been developing, it requires less and less oversight. I think this trend is just going to continue. At the end, the ultimate prompt is "to achieve goals." The system should be able to learn on its own, figure out all the problems, ask for directions when needed — just as humans would — and achieve those goals.

系统的 scaling law：公司从未真正解决过的问题

A (continued): Related to the system side, I wanted to throw this out as an interesting thought experiment. When you think about systems, there's this other angle of scaling up. So far we have scaled up models in terms of size and compute, etc. Now we are facing another axis — which is the scaling of systems. These systems today are composed of people and agents. So each axis is the number of agents, where some of them are people and some of them are models — they have different properties.

But the question is really: how do you get a favorable scaling of a system?

Companies scale, and their productivity goes down with scale. So people don't have a favorable scaling. Your company size goes 10x and your productivity is like 1.2x. That's not great. And today, agents are also not that amazing in terms of being able to actually scale them in a way that you can see that productivity of the entire system grows.

So the companies of the future — where you would want to be able to throw compute at problems and scale up agents and see that, let's say, when you double the number of agents you get to the same goal two weeks faster — that's the question. How do you save time?

The real future, the real thing that the world is fighting for, is how to get to a point faster. All the competition between companies — a lot of these companies are willing to pay 10x more compute to just get to something one month faster than others. You have to solve the scaling problem.

One interesting thing that we know about scaling laws is that if you want to get scaling laws right, you have to start small and get the scaling right before you scale up — otherwise things are not going to work. So I think of ourselves as the experiment, running experiments at a small scale, thinking about how can we get favorable scaling and then scale up the system.

Q: What aspects of the system do you think need to be solved in order for this to work better? Is it communication…?

A: Oversight — a lot of oversight issues. I think a lot of resource allocation problems. If you have the same compute, but you increase the number of researchers, how do you decide which ideas would get prioritized, etc.?

Q: This is what really happens in human researchers.

A: Yeah. Also with AIs. So there's a lot of technical problems to solve there that are very interesting.

Q: So now we're talking inter-agent politics — it's like "why did that agent get 1,000 views, I get only two?"

A: At the end of the day, with agents and with people, incentives are very important. And this is orthogonal to the performance of the agent itself.

Q: Because I know you've worked a lot on long-running agents and narrow slices of problems — but these things you're talking about are actually orthogonal to individual agent performance.

历史类比：我们活在另一个 1920 年代

A: One thing I want to highlight is such an interesting and impactful time that we're living in. There are these histories of science — for example, physics — a whole bunch of really, really impactful work happened in the 1920s and 30s. And then there's a little bit of a blank space. I think we're going through a similar thing in intelligence, and it's improving at a very fast rate. It's just a really interesting time to live.

Q: Do you think this sort of self-acceleration is already starting to happen — meaning when you look at the latest releases from the big labs — is the slope getting steeper because of this?

A: Absolutely. It has started in some sense and small ways when the coding models became truly useful. And it has picked up by every generation of new models. It improves everyone's productivity by itself, but that also improves the time to the next breakthrough, the next model.

Q: This is sort of a unique aspect of AI, isn't it? You have these sort of cheat codes. Coding is kind of a cheat code because a lot of the system is built on code. What you guys are doing is almost the next level of cheat code, because the actual experiments are sort of — you know, which you don't see with, like, when the internet gets faster, you don't recursively get faster internet. So it's kind of an interesting thing.

终局想象：科学繁荣，而不是失业潮

Q: Where do you think it all ends up?

A: It's a wide spectrum of outcomes. But our preferred outcome is just generally prosperity, specifically scientific prosperity — in the sense that a lot of the science is just unknown at the moment, and a lot of the engineering projects are also just bottlenecked by people being able to design things well, and just the intelligence being the bottleneck. We hope that some of the progress can translate into just us knowing a lot, lot more about ourselves and the universe.

Your question is maybe also one of the other reasons that pushed me to start a company. I think the current picture that's being painted about the impact of AI is not that positive — like automating people's jobs away. That doesn't seem like an exciting future. So I am trying in a way to focus on building a different one that has more positive outcome for everyone. And we think accelerating science is pure good for humanity.

You could decide where to put — you're building something powerful — you could decide where to put it at work. And I think we should feel like we are in charge and we can build the future we want.

These problems — like solving Alzheimer's disease, being able to predict it much in advance and solving it — these are super challenging problems. There are so many things that have to be solved on the path of it. [Solving them] would show everyone that AI — it should have been used this way from the beginning. But somehow we are losing focus on — these are the important problems that have to be solved, and smart people should be working on these problems.

So we want to change [that] — at least we can choose what we work on, and we want to be focused on creating this powerful technology and directing it at these problems that are longstanding. It will still take a lot of resources. It would create jobs, because you have to solve all other aspects of it that are not intelligence-related. Bottlenecks are going to move away from intelligence. So it's interesting to direct it at these problems that are just not about day-to-days, but solving problems that help everyone.

工具箱观：预训练不是全部

Q: And is it fair to say that continuing to scale up pre-training is probably not sufficient to solve these kinds of problems you're talking about? Because you're almost talking about generating net-new knowledge — which, I guess, is hard no matter how much pre-training you do.

A: In some sense, I think of pre-training, post-training, and all the paradigms that we have of using compute effectively as tools in my toolbox. At certain points in time, some tool is effective, and for the fixed amount of compute that we have, we go for the best tool.

Q: But what you're trying to build is not even just adding compute, but the system of putting expert people into the system as a whole. This is part of what amplifies, I think you're saying.

A: Yeah. So let's do this thought experiment of the way things are going. Our best hope is that we're going to have models that are going to be as good as our best scientists, or maybe become better than the best scientists. But how is that going to solve Alzheimer's disease for us?

These are problems that — we don't even know if it's possible, we don't even know what are the limits that exist. So we really need to move at much higher speed. If you're just thinking about slightly higher — building an AI scientist, etc. — this is not going to solve these problems.

Alzheimer's disease, for example, has so much structure in terms of data that you have to use, etc., that you cannot even see how in 10 years existing models would be able to move things much faster. With the current speed, we are not going to get there anytime soon. So we are impatient about solving those problems.

Q: Awesome. Well, this is awesome, guys. Thank you so much for working on this and for coming on the podcast.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：自加速 AI 的真正瓶颈不是模型，而是"公司形态"
表面上 Mirandil 在做技术——自加速 AI、AI for AI research。但听完整段对话，最锋利的洞见其实埋在 Venom 那句几乎被一带而过的话里：**"这种颠覆性技术很难在已有公司里生长，因为很多基本元素必须被重新定义。"**
这是一个被低估的判断：他们不是因为"技术更强"才离开 Anthropic，而是因为意识到——**一家以"训练大模型并按 token 收费"为商业模式的公司，在结构上无法把"让别人也能训练自己的模型"这件事做成**。这不是道德问题，不是 CEO 问题，而是**激励几何学**问题。就像让石油公司主导核聚变商业化一样，无论高管多么真诚，组织的引力场都会把它拉回旧轨道。
这意味着 AI 行业即将发生的不是技术更替，而是**公司物种的更替**——一种全新的"AI 原生公司"形态正在出现，它的内部细胞（员工角色、汇报关系、定价模型）从第一天起就为"AI 在做 AI 研究"这个事实而设计。
## 二、跨领域连接：他们在重演 1920 年代物理学，而不是 2010 年代的软

item.title: [Latent Space] The Agent Cloud: Databricks’ Bet on the Future of AI — Matei Zaharia and Reynold Xin
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的真正主角：不是 Agent，而是"会话状态"

大多数人看 Omnigent 会本能地把它归类为"另一个 agent 框架"——但这是表层误读。真正被悄悄重写的，是"session"作为一等公民的概念。

注意 Reynold 那个吊诡的红灯故事：他开车去看医生，每到红灯就打开笔记本看 agent 跑得怎么样。这不是段子，这是一个计算范式从"进程"迁移到"会话"的临床症状。在过去 50 年里，程序员关心的状态边界是"进程""容器""请求"；从这一刻起，边界变成了"一段可被多人、多设备、多时点接力的对话"。Omnigent 的 API 看起来朴素到可笑——发消息、收流、取消——但这恰恰是 HTTP 当年的样子。朴素的 API 配上正确的状态模型，往往就是下一代基础设施的入场券。

跨领域连接：这其实和 React 当年的洞见同构——UI 的难点从来不是渲染，而是"状态的归属和同步"。Omnigent 把 Vercel/Next.js 当年对前端做的事，对 agent 做了一次。

二、最反直觉的发现：Databricks 把"安全"做成了性能优化器，而不是限速器

通常我们把 security 想象成"减号"——它让东西更慢、更难用、更烦人。但 Matei 说的 contextual policy 偷偷做了一件激进的事：他们把安全策略变成了 stateful function，本质上就是一台微型解释器在 agent 的事件流上跑。

这意味着 policy engine 拥有的信息比 agent 本身还多——它知道这个 session 已经读了多少机密文档、装了多少天龄的 npm 包、烧了多少 token。一旦 policy 拥有"足够多的上下文"，它就不再是"拒绝/允许"的二元开关，而成了资源调度器。能花多少钱（"sub-agent 上限 $5"）、能信任到什么程度、能放行到哪一层——这些原本属于"运维"和"FinOps"的决策，被压进了同一个状态机。

这里有个出人意料的角度：Unity Catalog（数据治理）和 Omnigent（agent 治理）的本质完全相同——都是"对一条数据/动作流挂多个 stateful policy"。Matei 花了五年把数据治理做透，然后几乎是无缝把同一架构搬到了 agent 上。这不是偶然，这是他职业生涯第二次解同一道题，而第二次解决方案的精致度远胜第一次。这恰好是他自己提到的"second system syndrome"的反面——当你已经解过一次，第二次反而是真正的杰作。

三、隐藏的系统真相：LakeBase 把数据库变成了 Agent 的"长期记忆"，而行业还在讨论 RAG

OLTP/OLAP 的合并听起来像一个枯燥的工程话题，但 Reynold 自己都承认"昨晚才被澳大利亚客户说服"——这个时间差很重要。

真正的洞察是：当 agent 成为业务主角，OLTP 和 OLAP 之间那条延迟边界，从"分析师的耐心问题"变成了"agent 的智商问题"。 一个 agent 在排查 SLA 事件时，如果它能直接 join 实时订单流和历史日志，它的有效推理能力比"等 CDC 跑完再说"的 agent 高一个数量级。延迟成为智商的乘数。

跨领域连接：这和神经科学里 working memory 与 long-term memory 之间的检索延迟，是同一个数学问题。人类大脑的厉害之处不在于哪部分更强，而在于两者之间几乎零延迟的检索通路。LakeBase 在做的，本质上是给 agent 安装大脑的胼胝体——而行业里大多数公司还在做"给 agent 加更大的海马体（向量库）"。

四、彩蛋观察：Databricks 的真正护城河，是"愿意被自己内部用户骂"

整集里最被低估的一段：Reynold 自己写 wishlist，Matei 自己被 agent 烦得睡不着，Ali 在产品里手把手用。所有的产品决策都不是来自 PRD，而是来自"我作为重度用户的当周愤怒"。

Databricks 的真正神秘之处不是技术，是它保持了一家 1.5 万亿估值公司里几乎不可能存在的"创始人即首席使用者"密度。当工程师可以不写设计文档、不开 kickoff 会，直接 prototype 一个改写存储格式的实验然后被采纳——这个组织的反应速度跟一个 20 人创业公司是一样的。这才是为什么他们能在 Snowflake 之前 ship LakeBase、在 Cursor 还在做 IDE 时 ship Omnigent 的真正原因。

反常识结论：在 AI 时代，护城河不是模型，不是数据，甚至不是开源生态——而是"组织内部还有多少人是愿意为产品掉头发的真用户"。

Omnigent 的起源：两条线汇合

"I do think that a lot of people are exploring this sort of meta harness concept. What led you to it?"

"Yeah. There were actually a couple of converging lines, which is I think a good sign that you need something new. So, on the one hand, there's all the coding agent infra internally. We have a really great dev infra team. They built something called Isaac that's basically like a wrapper on Claude Code and Codex and lets you use them either on the web in like sandboxes or just on your dev machine or on your laptop or whatever. And then you know, they were adding all kinds of stuff there and we saw all the more advanced engineers were building their own workflows with tons of agents and they were building their own UIs and stuff on top of even on top of that."

"And then the other one was that like us building agents, we shipped this like data science agent called Genie on the research team which I co-lead basically. We also build a lot of internal ones for various things and then we have all the customer ones, and all of them were running into this thing of like, 'oh I need to switch model and harness and so on every few months.' Plus the agent is like completely useless if you can't share sessions with someone and have history and have search and all this layer on top of it for collaboration."

"I thought a bit about it from both contexts and at first people thought it was weird and like 'why are you doing coding agents and custom agents in the same thing?' but I said it's basically the same problems and you just want to build the stuff that lets you deliver the agent, maybe control it if you care about security, and make it portable across things. And then we prototyped some things as experiments. We said, yeah, actually we can make it work, and then we sort of built it for real."

类比：网络协议与数据共享

"I'm wondering if this kind of let's call it architecture maps to anything in your careers in the past. You know, like I always think about how a lot of things actually just tie back to operating systems."

"Mhm."

"A lot of operating systems tie back to databases or the other way around."

"So the thing— I do think it ties a lot to network protocols, you know, internet protocol. We also did— Yeah, we did stuff with like data sharing also which probably most viewers probably won't know unless they—"

"Yeah, open protocol is the term for sharing. Open sharing."

"Open sharing. Yeah, so it's like you have a company, you maintain some kind of table— let's say like Walmart or something. They have the inventory and what's been sold in each store. And then you also have suppliers and they would love to produce more things and ship them like exactly the moment you need them. So they would love real-time access to your table. So instead of sending emails around or Excel sheets or phone calls, why can't you share a view of that table in real-time with them? Then they query, they join it with their data and they decide what to send."

"So it's one of these things where you— you might ask like 'today since we can vibe code anything so fast, why do we even need to design protocols or APIs or software? Why can't you just vibe code things on demand?' But actually for this type of interoperability where multiple parties that are moving at different speeds are building stuff and you still want some layer on top to coordinate, you do want to design it and build it. So it reminds me of that— like agents talking to each other and users talking to agents and tools."

And then I wrote actually a document for my case that, 'Here's my wish list of what the actual environment should do.' And I think he actually ended up almost implementing every single one of them."

"Yeah, I remember Reynold saying— cuz my first prototype of this had just chats with your agent and he said, 'I have to be able to open a shell, like my own shell, and like list files and tail them and stuff.' So I was—"

"Uh-huh. Yes."

"'Give me a way to see my markdown files and render them properly. I don't need a separate tool anymore.' Yeah. I think— You also built that in."
