People are just trying to wrap their heads around how the agent's going to be used, and haven't made very much progress on identity.

Zico: I think so far, in a lot of cases, we are still operating on the condition that your agent has your permissions. That is a very standard default. Your permissions may be in a sandbox, but still — kind of your permissions. That will change in the very near future, because it has to. That mindset is going to change. It's not a product we offer right now, but getting into that space is certainly something we may be doing in the future.

Host: I'm curious about the shape of this. Is it that I have my twin — that is my delegate on all these things? Or do I need one for every app? That's exhausting.

Matt: Absolutely exhausting. And then one of the bigger challenges people will face when they start to roll out agent-identity solutions: you run into that same usability problem. What's the real recourse if it stopped, it can't do something? Okay, now it can do it if it has my explicit consent.

Host: And then people just get inured into giving it consent to everything.

Matt: And then agent-to-agent, you can do privilege escalation if you're not careful.

Zico: In terms of how this will evolve — actually I don't think it'll be per-app. I think what will happen first is people have different personas. You don't want your work life and your home email to be mixed up. A lot of bad things can happen, or that does. We are very good as humans at separating out lives — work life, home life, different work lives. Agents are not very good at that right now. They're terrible — exceedingly bad at this.

Host: It's the people making them have no work-life balance — why would you expect the agents to have any?

Zico: I think the way this will first develop: easy ways of switching between "here's a set of my accounts and apps I allow in this one agent; here's a set in another." This will evolve to be more fine-grained over time. If I were to make a prediction about how this would evolve, that's the most natural thing.

Host: Makes sense. Just profiles for everyone.

2026 趋势预测

Host: I think that is the rough scope. Is there any part of the story you're looking forward to for the rest of this year — an emerging trend for 2026?

Zico: There's lots of emerging trends — I can go on at length.

Host: Let's start with Gray Swan.

Zico: So far when we talk about our product offerings, we obviously work with a lot of the large labs. We're with a lot of enterprise though too. What's happening, and the scaling we're going to see: the abilities that were mainly front-of-mind for large labs (how do I ensure security of my agents, how do I ensure the models follow the policies I want) — those are going to become front-of-mind for everyone, for all enterprise, as they adopt tools like Codex, Claude Code, OpenClaud.

That's where our expansion is going. A lot of the intention behind our Series A is explicitly to take the technology we've been developing in conjunction with both enterprise and the large labs, and really scale deployments on enterprise. What I see happening in the next year, from the Gray Swan side, is real growth in the number of non-AI companies deploying this technology, because it becomes central to their operations.

研究方向

Research-wise — I've already talked about some. The science: let's start with the science of AI. We always want to do other sciences — let's do AI for physics — let's just start with AI science. That needs a lot of work right now.

That's what I'm most excited about. Understanding models better, but doing it through the power of agents.

Matt 的乐观

Matt: One thing I've been very encouraged by, for really only the past two or three months, and the pace at which this has happened has been increasing: people who start to build an agent, and don't take it all the way to "we finished, we think it's great, now it's in front of customers or the entire organization." They have this epiphany before they get there — that whatever prompts I put, like, I need a solution here. I understand there are real risks. I understand this is a weird, interesting, and really capable model I'm working with — but if I don't put more measures in place to make sure it stays safe and behaves the way I want, things will go wrong.

People coming to us proactively, knowing they need a real solution — that's very encouraging. It's a sign of agents kind of landing outside of just frontier labs and the research community and scientists. People are starting to get it. Looking forward to all the amazing apps people are going to build on top of these models — and the security that will help them stand up.

Arena 中的企业用户

Host: Is there a future where your customers are part of the arena? Because these are basically your... right, these are independent entities. There's a guy in Australia who's your number one. But at some point you have the network effect where you start having enterprise use cases inside of this.

Matt: You mean testing enterprise deployments inside the arena. We have had the situation where people join the arena — they're maybe cyber security professionals, they get interested in AI security, they come across the arena, and then eventually they become a customer when their organization needs a solution.

Host: How often does that happen?

Matt: Not a huge number of times. But there are a lot of thoughtful people from a cyber security background who have found their way there. Enterprises are always going to be more paranoid about putting their custom agent — pre-deployment, still in development — up on this public platform for anybody to come hit. What we have done is worked to make private arenas, where some subset of the contestants we've handpicked —

Host: Oh, NDA.

Matt: Yeah.

Host: And what do they work on? What was the class of problem that would require a private arena?

Matt: Pretty much any enterprise application — that's the point. Enterprises are not willing to put up their pre-deployment agents on the arena for the general public to come at. They're fine if it's 20 people we've handpicked from the arena.

参与者收益

Host: For listeners who might be interested — what do I make as a participant? What's on the table here?

Matt: For public competitions we communicate a pricing and incentive structure up front, and it differs for each arena. Designing the right set of incentives to get people focused on finding useful vulnerabilities and problems — without reward hacking or finding de minimis things — is tricky.

Host: Are you human-judging the reward hacks if they happen?

Matt: Sometimes. Well — we have a lot of automated graders. But ultimately if they can beat all those graders, there's a human who can take a look. We work with UKC and Casey and so forth — they'll come in and work as independent judges and evaluators and lend their expertise.

Host: So you're a community that any enterprise can call on — that's really useful data. It's almost like Mercor for red teaming.

Matt: Yeah.

AI 保险与 AIUC

Host: One of our upcoming guests is on the other side of this — the AI underwriting company. I don't know if you've come across them.

Zico: Yeah.

Host: What do you think of that market?

Zico: Such an interesting market, and I think it pairs extremely well with our model. How do you assess the risk of a company's AI deployment? Well, use a tool like SHADE, or use the arena. We've done a lot of work with them exactly for that.

If a company finds a level of risk but wants — because they can't be insured because they're too risky — to reduce their risk, what do you do? I mean, look, we shouldn't be the only provider here. But what do you do? Well, you put safety systems around your model, including things like Cygnal.

So it pairs extremely well, because in some sense we can be a — I want to emphasize, we're not getting there yet, this is hypothetical — but we can be an authorized partner with them, so they can do more than just say "hey, you're uninsurable." They can both assess it more rigorously with tools like SHADE, and prescribe mitigations when there are problems, using tools like Cygnal.

It's an incredibly good fit. They also bring us customers, because a lot of customers — yes, there's the risk of bad things happening (that's actually driving probably most of our current business) — but also the risk of "you want to have insurance about when things go wrong, you want to be compliant." Being out of compliance is also a risk, and we can address that too.

Matt: AIUC is fantastic, and they got on it very early. The parallel to cyber insurance is so clear. When you apply for cyber insurance, you have to document what measures are in place — what do I have for detection and response? Structurally, they must have an arm's-length, third-party. They cannot do what you do.

Zico: We do explicitly work with them. If they have somebody they want to evaluate —

Host: You already work with them. I'm curious why you say you're not there yet.

Zico: There's not a full compliance framework that is universally accepted by regulators, say. We still have a ways to go between —

Host: Between where we are and when we get to something like cyber, like SOC2.

Zico: Well, SOC2 is a voluntary industry thing.

Host: It is, but it has some issues — it stems from being more the product of, less of cyber experts and more of accountants.

Zico: CPAs. So SOC2 is not a great model, we'll just say. But it is a model. Conceptually, something like that — when I say we're not there yet, I mean we're not at that point yet with AI insurance. We are very much there in terms of conceptually assessing risk, and then offering ways to mitigate that risk.

Matt: One of the things I do think about AIUC — they have made a good first attempt at something like a compliance framework. They came to us, came to others from academia and the startup community, and tried to ground it in real technical issues and how you might mitigate those. That direction definitely has legs.

Host: What would you want to see from them? We're going to have them next. Like — would you want them to fully establish a SOC2, a Sarbanes-Oxley? There's different levels of legal bindingness.

Zico: SOC2 is not legally binding in any sense.

Host: It is an industry standard. Kind of like a passport where you got it, okay, cool, you did the bare minimum.

Matt: Yep, and if you don't, then it's going to be very painful to go through procurement.

I myself would be curious about what the demand looks like. Why do you get cyber insurance? You get cyber insurance because you have to carry it if you want to get this enterprise deal, or you have a genuine concern. There are lots of different pressure factors. I'd be curious where we are on the timeline of — why do people come to AIUC? What's driving them to seek out AI agent insurance?

Host: I mean, the first major publicly-in-the-news prompt-injection breach — they'll probably do it. The largest I know is some hertz got injected, some airline got injected, but nothing big.

Zico: The name Gray Swan is in reference to black swan events — things no one could see coming. A gray swan is an unlikely event you can kind of see coming. That's where we are with all this. This is going to happen. We know it's coming. It's not going to shock anyone when it happens. But this is the time — you want to get ahead of it while you can.

Matt: People don't always publicize when it happens either. We know that it has happened and has caused real damage — that's the factor that has driven some people to us. They want protection from that.

结语

Host: Amazing. Well — thank you for fighting a good fight. I'm sure we'll check back in over the years as you develop and hopefully solve this. It'll never be solved — but we'll solve it by fully understanding the models. I do like automating AI research.

Zico: Yeah.

Host: Okay. Well, thank you so much.

Zico: Great for having us.

Matt: Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：红队模型已经在"小范围"上超越人类，但人类仍是"分布外"专家
Ziko 在访谈中悄悄地丢出一颗炸弹：他们的 SHADE 系统在最近的对抗赛中已经"quite a bit better than humans"。但真正令人意外的并不是这一句话本身，而是它揭示的一个悖论——**前沿大模型几乎不能做红队**。因为安全训练让它们拒绝攻击行为；它们越"对齐"，越笨拙。这意味着：**红队能力与模型规模脱钩，与模型的"越界倾向"挂钩**。
这指向一个被业界忽视的结构性事实：安全与能力在 scaling law 上是正交的。模型再大，对 prompt injection 的抵抗力并不会自动变强（论文里那张 scatter plot 几乎是平的）。这是整个 AI Safety 商业化故事的隐藏支点——如果安全能随规模自然涌现，Gray Swan 这种公司就不会存在。
## 二、跨领域连接：从"机制可解释性"到"AI 保险"，本质都是同一件事——把不可见的风险变成可定价的对象
表面上，Ziko 谈了三个看似无关的话题：mech interp 的科学化、SOC2 合规、网络

item.title: [a16z Deep Dives] Teaching AI the Language of Design | Deep Dives with a16z
item.dc:creator: a16z Deep Dives
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：设计师比工程师更会"驯服"AI

视频中最锋利的一刀来自 Paul：同一个 Claude、同一个网站、同一套设计系统，设计师得到的输出始终优于工程师。差别不在模型，而在词汇——"vertical rhythm"、"negative space"、"quieter"、"bolder"，这些是工程师从不使用的"咒语"。

这意味着一个被严重低估的事实：Prompt 工程的瓶颈不是逻辑能力，而是领域词汇的密度。LLM 时代真正的"特权阶层"不是会写代码的人，而是手里握着精确隐喻系统的人——诗人、爵士乐手、香水调配师、武术教练。他们的"行业黑话"在前 AI 时代是壁垒，在后 AI 时代是杠杆。Impeccable 本质上是把设计师的"黑话词典"塞进了 agent harness，等于给所有人发了一本"魔法咒语手册"。

二、跨领域连接：紫色渐变 → Tailwind 默认值 → 路易十四的紫袍

Paul 指出 AI 生成的 UI 普遍是"紫色渐变"，根源是 Tailwind 的默认主题里有紫色。这是一个微观的"默认值暴政"案例。但 John 在结尾把它推到了宏观——"设计在欧洲语境下源自皇室对稀缺材料的独占欲"：紫色之所以高贵，是因为提取一克泰尔紫染料需要碾碎上万只海螺。

把这两条线接起来你会看到一个吓人的对称： - 路易时代：紫色稀缺 → 紫色 = 权力的标记 - 2023 AI 时代：紫色廉价（一个 CSS class）→ 紫色 = AI 的标记

同一种颜色，从"贵族签名"沦为"机器签名"，跨越四百年只用了一个 CSS 框架的默认值。 这揭示了一个更深的规律：当材料从稀缺变为无限时，"品味"的语义会被翻转——从"我能用上"变成"我能避开"。未来的精英不再炫耀他们使用了什么，而是炫耀他们删除了什么。Impeccable 的"anti-attractor"机制，本质上是在帮人类完成一次"反向贵族化"。

三、一个尚未被广泛察觉的范式转移：UX → AX，以及"打字机时代的工匠运动"将重演

John 抛出 AX（Agentic Experience） 的概念——robots.txt、llms.txt、--help、错误信息，这些"非视觉界面"将成为下一代设计的主战场。Agent 才是新的用户。

但更值得警觉的是他随口埋的那枚炸弹：这场转变会复刻 1880 年代英国的 Arts and Crafts 运动。当蒸汽机吞掉手工业后，威廉·莫里斯一代反而把"手工的痕迹"重新定义为奢侈。今天 Claude/Codex 正在做同样的事——它会把 80% 的设计工作"机械化"，于是剩下 20% 的"人造痕迹"会变成新的奢侈品。

这里有一个让我意外的判断：Paul 提到他做的精美 slider 动画"常常不被高层看见"。在前 AI 时代，这是匠人之痛；在后 AI 时代，这恰恰是匠人的护城河——因为只有人类高层才需要靠"看见这种细节"来证明自己仍是人类。一个隐藏的预测：未来三年，CEO 们会重新雇佣大量"无法被 agent 描述其价值"的设计师，作为一种"非功利性身份消费"。这与 19 世纪贵族雇佣画师、20 世纪 CEO 雇佣建筑师同构。

四、最锋利的洞见（隐藏在对话末尾）：LLM 学的是"输出"，不是"输入"

Paul 那句"LLM 是基于人类的输出训练的，不是基于输入"几乎是一句哲学宣言。模型看到的是决策的结果，永远看不到决策发生时的内部状态——犹豫、否决、那句"不，再来一次"。

这解释了一个长期困惑：为什么 AI 永远在"approximation of taste"上徘徊？因为真正的品味是一个减法函数，而训练数据里只有加法历史。Impeccable 的真正贡献，是它第一次试图把"减法的语言"注入 agent——这是品味第一次以可执行代码的形式被外化。

📝 转录文本

Teaching AI the Language of Design | Deep Dives with a16z

开场引子：几个核心论点的预告

Designers when using Claude as opposed to engineers using Claude would consistently get better results, and it's because of the language that they use.

We have to remember that design in the European sense came from royalty and the desire to be distinctive because they were working with scarce materials. What's interesting about this era is that this idea of taste doesn't fit when all the materials are available to everyone.

Right now everybody's trying to solve whether LLMs have taste. These models have millions of definitions of taste. LLMs have been trained on the output of humanity, not on the input. So what led to a design decision is not something that the LLMs know.

Maybe advice for our design engineers in the room: how have you in the past effectively communicated the value of an instinct versus a deadline, or even another instinct which may be less informed? Yeah, that's a tough one.

一、设计与技术的关系：一个老问题，一个新时刻

主持人： I want to actually talk a little bit about, you know, perhaps to begin with the relationship between design and technology. There are some people who may view design as sort of more spiritually close to art, and others who see it perhaps in a more utilitarian fashion — you know, the design of everyday things. So perhaps John, I can sort of ask you to kick us off and talk to us about how you see the interplay between the two.

John: Well, first of all, glad to be here. I used to think about this a lot when I was at Kleiner Perkins and thinking like, why is it that design is important in 2014, 2015? Like, why was it important? It was because this weird company called Airbnb was unusually successful.

And if you connect to why design became important, it was because of mobile. Before mobile, desktop experiences could be crappy and it was okay because you didn't use them very often. But mobile had high usage, and therefore it was bad all the time. It would be painful. So that's when it sort of started to happen.

In terms of the relationship now, however, I'm so excited to be on this with Paul because I've been a fan of this moment when we'd be able to auto-design. And that's since the 90s when I was at MIT. We thought it was going to be possible one day, and now it's very possible.

主持人: Thanks. And Paul, as someone who's really at the cutting edge of all that — incredible. So, elaborate a bit for us: what do you mean by auto-design? And, you know, does the way that it works today match the sort of idea that you had back in it?

Muriel Cooper 与 auto-design 的史前史

John: Well, I mean, if you go back to the 1980s, there was a woman named Muriel Cooper, who was unusual because she was a trained print graphic designer. She designed those — uh, you know — MIT Press. There's a beautiful logo at MIT Press. She designed that, among other pretty major things in MIT's history and publication.

She was the one who imagined a world where people wouldn't want to use the terminal and might want to use this thing called Helvetica to look at things on the screen. So she predicted the whole desktop publishing revolution, electronic publishing revolution.

And at the time she also had a bunch of people from MIT's AI Lab. MIT AI Lab was asking, how do we create things by machines that humans are good at? And so her lab focused on how to automatically design things with quote-unquote "AI" of the time.

主持人: Incredible. Paul, give us your view on the relationship and interplay between design and technology. Has it turned out the way you expected?

Paul：设计师/工程师的混合身份

Paul: I think we're still working that out, to be honest. But I do see a lot of it converging in interesting ways.

I remember seeing one of John's talks about algorithmic design a long time ago at some conference, and I always thought that — you know — I was always this oddball that was both designer and engineer, and I never wanted to really be boxed in into any of those areas.

But I think the reality is that it's now much easier to build yourself tools that help you with that, right? That help you algorithmically create design and supercharge your design with technology. And I think that's really exciting. So, to me, they're one and the same. They've always have been.

I mean, John calls it auto-design. I think we're basically like — to use John's words as well — we're kind of like raising the floor in areas that can be mechanical. And I think that's really exciting because so much of design is spent with the things that we can automate, as opposed to doing like the really high-level thinking of what actually needs to be done, what needs to be built. And that's something that I'm really excited about.

二、Craft vs. Automation：编辑的艺术

主持人: Paul, do you think there's a tension between craft and automation in design?

Paul: Yeah, I think so. I think ultimately everybody has to think for themselves how much they want to automate away. There is definitely still the intent and the judgment that is needed from a human side — I would say — on figuring out the what and what not to do.

I actually really greatly respect people like Steve Jobs, because they are the greatest — I mean, Steve was one of the greatest editors in the world, right? He said no to so many things, as opposed to saying yes to so many things. And I think having a viewpoint is still important.

So I think ultimately the machine can work for you, but the same way you take a photo on a good camera — ultimately you figure out what you want to take a photo of. There's a lot of post-processing in the camera, right? There's so much. I mean, if you look at an iPhone camera today, there's so much happening on device. That photo that you took is literally not reality anymore, right? But there's still some sort of human viewpoint.

设计师作为"人类体验的建筑师"

主持人: John, maybe to bring it to you — what do you think are the limitations of the sort of, you know, the Cloud Code, Codex, and all the agents today when it comes to design expression?

John: Oh wow. Well, I mean, everyone's saying it's limitless, and it's that — not the Cambrian period, blah blah blah. There's so many adjectives like, "Oh my gosh, amazing."

I think the reality is that this is — uh — been a long time coming. In the same way, we saw writing being automated before LLMs, and now we're seeing — and we saw marketing assets being automated as well. So we just spread that to the kind of — we went upstream on this idea of designing things, which, to Paul's point, requires restraint.

It requires all kinds of things which are the opposite of engineering. Speaking as an engineer as well — if I can make it, I'mma build it. Oh, I'll do this. I'll keep doing this and this and this and this. And so restraint isn't part of the equation —

主持人: Unless you're an architect.

John: Designers always think like architects of the human experience. Now, with the harness and the models coming together, we have a nice combo platter, plus the evals. We have the feedback loop that's actually really brand new — and that's why we're so excited.

We've been experimenting with Paul. We have this thing called the GitHub Copilot app. It's a new app. And the challenge with that has been to keep the human taste and craft and all the high-tech goodies. And to Paul's point about raising the floor — humans should be able to raise the ceiling of what they can do. So we have human animators making the craft and the final decisions around some of the super-duper highs, extreme polish, but we want to have the coding agents do the part that have the drudgery.

三、Impeccable 的起源：一个为自己造的工具

主持人: Yeah, that makes perfect sense. I want to pull the thread about the new Git product. But Paul, why don't we actually take a step back, and you can talk to us about Impeccable — what was the intention, what does it look like today? And then let's talk a bit about how you and John are working together.

Paul: Yeah, of course. So Impeccable started as an open-source project that I needed for myself. I used to contract for a couple of my friends in the last year to figure out how far can I push AI engineering, and how much can I 10x or 5x — or whatever — myself. And I know many are going through a journey right now.

In the process, I had to build a whole lot of tooling for myself. And one of those tools was Impeccable.

关键观察：设计师比工程师更会和 Claude 沟通

Ultimately, I noticed that whenever I would tell Claude or Codex, "make this better," it would do a really terrible job, because it didn't know what — what angle of better, what does better mean in the design context, right?

I also noticed that designers when using Claude, as opposed to engineers using Claude, would consistently get better results. So why is that? Right? The model is literally the same. The website is the same. The design system might be the same. But the designers still get better output, even though they're prompting a machine.

And it's because of the language that they use. It's because oftentimes engineers don't use words like vertical rhythm, or negative space, or make this bolder or quieter — like, they don't have the same vocabulary as a designer who's been in the game for a long time.

So the first instinct that I had was to bring that vocabulary to the actual harness, to the agent harness. And that was the first iteration of Impeccable. And that already made a huge difference.

一个意外的发现：设计师也欢迎它

Interestingly, I thought that it would alienate designers, and it would mainly appeal to engineers — but that was not the case. I think a lot of designers are being asked right now to move closer to code, and I think that makes a lot of sense. And maybe they're waking up themselves to that idea, because everybody's moving closer to code. Code is the substrate that the agents use, of course. And Impeccable feels like a way to get there much more quickly and with the language that they use.

So I would say it's actually — if I look at the target audience right now, it's half engineers, half designers, a whole bunch of PMs as well, founders, solopreneurs.

But that's how Impeccable came together. It is an agent skill that's completely open source. It has lots of sub-commands to steer, and then I kind of built a visual iteration mode on top of it, and a quality layer that removes slop — and of course we can talk about that too.

But it does stop the overfitting of the models. So not everything is "Claude beige," as I call it, or you know, instrument italics, or purple gradients. But yeah, it's ultimately these three pieces: it's a vocabulary, it's a quality layer, and it's a visual iteration mode that runs in your own codebase.

四、Kai's Power Tools、PostScript 与 Impeccable 的历史对照

主持人: Yeah, knowing the vocabulary — it's sort of like a set of magic spells, you know? Either you know them or you don't. And I love how Impeccable makes them more broadly available. John, talk to us a bit about the analog between what Paul's doing with Impeccable and Kai's Power Tools.

John: Well, in the late 1980s, there was this application that was called Photoshop, that was amazing because it suddenly let you draw pictures and play with photographs instead of whatever. It was popular among photographers and people like that.

But when something called Kai's Power Tools came out, it blew up the TAM for Photoshop, because people could do weird algorithmic things with their faces or all kinds of filters — because Adobe Photoshop opened up this plug-in architecture. So people could write code, could put things in that plug-in format.

And so when I saw Paul's work, it reminded me of Kai's Power Tools, but for design and less weird — but very focused, very functional.

PostScript 时刻

When I saw Impeccable, it reminded me of — just giving away my era — of when PostScript came out. Most people don't realize that PostScript was a miracle, because the Adobe guys, the Warnock — the manual for PostScript is beautiful. It's incredibly well-designed. Those engineers understood graphic design better than most graphic designers.

So PostScript properly encoded the perfect set of primitives to implement visual graphic design of the day. And so it required people who were technical and also graphic-design-minded. Same with Donald Knuth before that, with TeX, right? He was an incredible typographer, mathematician. So TeX was a miracle in the combination of function and form.

So when I saw Impeccable — not to embarrass Paul — I was like, whoa, this is like the PostScript moment. Someone actually gets that it's not just filters, it's also about subtraction, and it was all the different dimensions a designer might take. And so in that I saw a really skilled engineer-designer, and that got me excited.

五、GitHub Copilot 与 Impeccable 的合作

主持人: That's tremendous. That's so cool. Maybe John, talk a bit about how you guys are planning to work together.

John: Oh, well, I'm super excited because I recently picked up all of GitHub Design. And great timing, because we were launching this GitHub Copilot app that's already in GA. And it is different than things we've built in the past, in that — as you know — the craft bar is very high now for applications.

So we wanted to set the bar with this app. And when the head of product, Mario, said, "Hey, do you know this Impeccable thing?" — I was like, "Oh my gosh, it's one of my favorite skills." And so I reached out to Paul, and Paul said, "Hey, oh my gosh, you know, we kind of — maybe we know each other."

And so we had this wonderful conversation by text, and since then, a few weeks later, we're going to — Apollo's going to be the first one to be in our integrations of this powerful design skill, because we believe that design's important in the digital — what do you call it — harness age. And I think Paul has the best implementation of it.

主持人: And Paul, how do you sort of see the product showing up within the new Git — you know, Impeccable showing up within the new Git product?

Paul: First of all, I'm super excited to get Impeccable into the hands of more builders and solopreneurs and individual enterprises. And I think GitHub is obviously a great place to do that. Everybody's using GitHub today, and it's a great way to roll out Impeccable to more.

Building in the skill as a built-in tool is one thing. I think there's a lot more different directions that we can take from there, and that's what I'm really excited about.

The GitHub Copilot app has a "pick and polish" mode that allows you to pick and change something and quickly edit something — and of course Impeccable has its own implementation of that. And right now you need to opt in, and it's a bit clunky. I think through first-party harness integrations like the collaboration that I'm doing with GitHub, we can actually improve that significantly and make it so that it's a lot smoother.

There's nothing there to be announced yet just now, but I am super eager to work with John and Mario on actually improving the Impeccable and GitHub integration.

六、Slop 是一个移动的靶子

主持人: Okay. So follow-up question, Paul. Obviously Git is one of the most important platforms in the world, especially at this moment in time. So let's wind the clock forward and imagine a world in which many people are using the new GitHub product and Impeccable to have these sort of designs that are much improved from what you get default from the models. As those designs become in distribution for the next gen of models, do we then have to — does the next version of Impeccable have to steer in a different direction altogether? Or are these sort of evergreen best practices that live regardless of what's in or out of distribution?

Paul: Yeah, it's a great question. You know, that's already happening. That's the really interesting thing.

In 2022, 2023, everybody thought AI slop was purple gradients. And to be honest, the models aren't really doing that anymore. The models aren't producing purple gradients anymore — I mean, maybe like, you know, cheap models — but overall, they don't.

I think the world has moved on to beige backgrounds, tinted backgrounds, Instrument Serif, eyebrow text. You know, there are the typical AI tells that you look for now. If you use Claude design, oftentimes you run into a design like that, for example. But it's not unique to Claude design.

So how did this happen, right? We basically said, "okay, no purple gradients," and then the model steers into a different part of the latent space and just picks the next best thing.

Anti-attractors 与算法化的 IKEA

So ultimately, slop is a moving target. And every time you move to another part of the latent space and everybody adopts it, you get — I kind of like — algorithmic Uniqlo or IKEA, or something, right?

And now that's not necessarily bad. It doesn't mean the design is bad. It just means the design is not unique. And you want uniqueness if you're building something like a landing page that attracts people's attention.

So what I've tried to do with Impeccable — and I'm constantly updating those rules — but what I tried to do with Impeccable is to build these types of anti-attractors. For example, instead of just going to a different font to the overused fonts, it actually tries to create a random seed that picks something based on a small user interview with you, and maybe even some scripts that run that create randomness, to actually steer it into a completely different space of the latent space.

I do it with colors. I do it with fonts. I do it with a bunch of different things to create some sort of uniqueness. And there'll be more there that I'm going to do. But I really do want to make sure that not everything ends up looking the same.

紫色渐变与橙色 web 的"默认值"故事

I ended up doing that before, by the way. Like, the reason why purple gradients — do you know the reason why purple gradients are purple gradients?

主持人: Tell me.

Paul: Tailwind. So Tailwind's default theme has purple.

主持人: I see.

Paul: And because of the default theme, the purple gradients were introduced everywhere. That's at least the common saying right now. The common theory.

And ironically, I was at fault of coloring the web orange before.

主持人: Yeah.

Paul: And the reason why is because when I started jQuery UI — we'd created a theme framework, a theme roller kind of thing — and the default theme was orange. I don't know why orange. I'm not sure why I picked orange at the time, but I colored the whole web orange overnight. And I learned my lesson.

主持人: The power of defaults, huh?

Paul: Yes, for sure.

七、从 UX 到 AX：Agentic Experience

主持人: John, do you think uniqueness is an important attribute in design? And maybe broadly talk about your view on uniqueness and slop.

John: Well, as Paul was scrapping this up — I think we're in this era, because you can see all the new model companies that are building designer models or visual design models, whatever. We're at this era where design will be further commoditized and automated, so that a new kind of design emerges. I think that's the next wave that'll happen because of this.

In the Design and Tech Report I give every year for South by Southwest, I've been pushing — moving from UX to AX, Agentic Experience. And agentic experience is non-visual. It is the world of robots.txt. It's llms.txt. It's command line --help. It's a world where we're designing for agentic affordances instead of just visual affordances.

That force multiplication will occur once we can retire more of UX, I believe. But it'll require this sort of surge of automated design solutions that cover 80% of those cases. So 20% can be spent by real humans — but the humans will move on to AX, I believe.

主持人: Interesting. Can you say a bit more about it? I mean, what would be the role of human taste and judgment in AX?

Meta Design 与 Erik Spiekermann

John: Oh, well, the thing that every designer knows eventually is that — and actually there was a famous typographer, Erik Spiekermann, who designed a very popular sans-serif typeface called Meta. This is before Meta became a company name. Okay? And his company in San Francisco was called Meta Design. At the time, one of the top design firms in the world.

Meta Design speaks to the fact that design can either be the application of craft or the abstraction of craft. And so I think the abstraction of craft is, at the moment, what is the best way to design things.

There was a time where being a design craftsman meant you could take a picture of Helvetica, cut it with an X-Acto knife, put rubber cement on the back, and you were amazing. We moved to different higher-level primitives of the page etc. with desktop publishing. Now I think we're going to move even more meta — and so it means that designers are closer to programmers, but programmers of interaction.

And I think Paul probably — he subconsciously is working in this way — when we talk about evals, Paul, evals live in latent space construction as literal meta design.

Paul：仍然存在以人为中心的设计

Paul: I think that's true. I think there is a whole area of design here. I also think you're probably right with the evals.

The interesting thing with AX is that often times here you're designing for a different audience — and that's the agent, as opposed to the human. I think — I guess my viewpoint is that, that area of design will be increasingly important. I also think there's still the area of human-centric design as well.

And I think — I mean, to the point that we made at the very beginning — that is shifting towards higher-level work, right? Sort of the last 20% of what makes something unique.

It's interesting. I mean, we've seen so many AI startups that are trying to enable creative work, right? Whether it's music or video or whatever. But we haven't really seen the next Hayao Miyazaki from Runway or some other video startup, right? We haven't really — I think it's probably fair to say — I don't think we have seen a music AI startup's next, I don't know, Billie Eilish or Coldplay.

And why is that? I think a lot of focus right now is on raising the floor. And going back to that, I think raising the ceiling — there's too little time spent on that specifically. And I'm really excited about it. So I want to build tools that also cater to that. So kind of like both ends of the spectrum.

But I do think that the world is shifting towards higher-end experiences. The audience is demanding more too. That's the interesting — right, like if everything looks relatively polished and clean — you know, if we're raising the floor, and you have a Claude-designed website that looks Claude-designed but it's a legit website, right? It's a good enough website to get the job done — then the only differentiator will be whatever we craft on top of that.

I always thought that was very interesting. I spend a lot of time, like many of my design engineer friends, we spend a lot of time crafting something really unique and particular — sort of like, I don't know, like a slider that is beautifully animated, right? But oftentimes that work would drown. That work would not be seen by higher-ups, who, you know, have completely different business goals.

I think it's the moment for these people right now to shine — that's one of my theories. I think the craftsman that can kind of grasp the last 10%, 20% and really put a unique spin on something, I think that's also really interesting. So I do subscribe to AX, but I think there's also that next-level human experience that I'm very excited about.

八、Agent 会有"审美"吗？

主持人: Yeah, they're both interesting. On the agentic topic — where my mind goes is: will agents value aesthetics? Will they have their own definition? Because you could argue that something that is aesthetic, or appears to be high quality, there's information in that that we as humans value, and we make decisions at least in part accordingly. What will be the analog for agents, if anything at all?

I don't know. John, what do you think?

John: Speed, clarity, verbosity, and all the edge cases. So the best example I like is the one of — you have to have a really good --help, you have to have great error messages.

But — and Paul is correct — that the ceiling-raising can occur once we can wick away all the generic good-quality design, and that's going to be extremely exciting.

与 1880 年代 Arts and Crafts 运动的对照

It echoes the era of the Arts and Crafts movement of the late 1800s, when machines took over, humans developed this next level of craft. And so that will occur, and I think tools like what Paul is thinking of can enable that.

At the same time, from a functional perspective, there's so much runway on the AX side that I believe that primarily visual design thinkers will be able to crack that faster, because there's so many gnarly problems in it that an average engineering mind will not be the right shape mind, I believe.

API 与 CLI 设计：Agent 是新的用户

Paul: I think that's actually a good point. If I can respond to that real quick — I think a great example would be API design and also CLI design, to your point, right? You mentioned --help, but I'm hearing this over and over, and I agree with it: agents themselves are not really good at designing those interfaces yet.

So they're not really good at designing API interfaces, designing CLIs. And that's kind of ironic because they're the user for them now. But I do think that's right. I do think information architecture is another great example — it's a certain type of designer that's very good at crafting a good navigation.

John: And they have to think differently. Yeah, absolutely.

Paul: The design of Impeccable, to me, is an example of great sort of computational craft —

John: For people who understand how to use manipulations of agents and code. So that's why another thing I admired about your work — it's very computational.

九、本能 vs. 工具：是哪一边的问题？

主持人: Really interesting. But do you guys — is it an instinct problem then, in terms of raising the ceiling, or is it a tooling problem, or is it both? Because, you know, John, you said that the brain isn't quite shaped the right way, but we also have a tooling gap. So how much of it is instinct, how much of it is tools?

Paul: I would say it's probably a combination of the two. I think there's definitely a lot you can do with manual labor and manual craft that you still can't do with AI today, or with current tooling, I would say.

There's some CSS that's still manually written because the agents aren't good enough at it yet. But that's usually things like motion. So, you know, right now there's no good feedback loop for motion.

雨水向上飘的 shader：温度分辨率的缺失

I've built this shader library in the past, and I had this one shader that was animating rain upwards, after working with the model for a bit. And it's not the model's fault — the model just takes screenshots and it's like, "well, I guess that looks like rain." But it doesn't understand temporal resolution.

Now, Gemini does a little bit more than that — it samples frames on a video with sort of like one frame per second — but even that doesn't really give you the full picture. So it's still really, really limited. So there are areas of design like motion that are still super limited to what we can do today.

But I also think the bigger problem is that it's not that we can't build tools to accelerate those things. I think we totally can. It's just that we haven't yet. I think there's a lot of tools that can be built, can be explored here in the space, that really target craftsmen that want to go beyond, that really want to raise the ceiling.

So I think right now it's a pretty barren landscape for tools that really push the envelope, I would say. SUNO is actually a great example of something that started for, you know, beginners and sort of vibe sound designers, right, that wanted to just one-shot a quick song. But now it expanded very much into real music production, right? There are producers using it for all sorts of things now. They've built a whole studio around it, and they are really trying to push sort of the last 20% now, which is interesting. So they went through a big shift there. So I think that will happen more and more. I think right now it's still quite manual.

John：天花板永远不会被自动化（这是好消息）

主持人: Really interesting. John, how do you view instinct versus tooling?

John: Well, as Paul was saying that — and thanks for all these questions, I don't think about these meta questions a lot in my job.

Well, first off, the vision models don't really see the way we see. And there's not enough training data to help a vision model think like a designer can see — because there's so many dimensions in the latent space of a visual mind, still, that create a visual mind, versus looking for license plates and more functional things.

I think that also the number of people who will raise the ceiling will not be a high number, and it won't be that valuable in the mass market. So I think it'll never be automated — and that's good news for people who want to stay in the craft of this kind of work.

I feel like right now we're just making it easier for more people to produce average and above-average work, which is the lesson of task-based automation. But those people who are dying for the creativity to last — it'll always remain there. Only problem: the customer base will be smaller. For people who are in that, it's like — do we know letterpress? Letterpress printing? Oh my gosh, I love it. But not many people will pay for it. So it's that market mechanics.

"数字主街"与小 TAM 产品

主持人: Yeah, it's really, really interesting. I mean, one of the things I believe — you know, Paul and I have spoken about this, John — is that we're going to have a lot more products with small but still compelling markets. You know, the million-dollar TAM products, the $100,000 TAM products, because they were economically infeasible before. So we can sort of imagine a world where it's more of a digital Main Street — or a digital homesteading — that can support a product that may have smaller scale but be more opinionated, and be that last 10%.

John: If I may add to that — the thing that I feel over and over is that the reason why someone is willing to pay that much more for that bespoke better thing is they're paying for human trust and accountability. So for brands and products where people demand that level of, like, "can I trust this," that human ability, that human smell could become even more valuable than ever before.

十、John 2010 年那条早了五年的预言

主持人: Yeah, I love that. Actually, John, this is a good moment — I want to read a quote, this is you, Forbes 2010:

The software industry is poised to embrace its craft heritage. By 2020, software will return to a cottage industry with bespoke apps made by many. We will discover the value of authorship.

I mean, seems like you might have been a few years early in your prediction, but it seems like we're getting there now. I mean, is that how you see the world playing out? Many more small apps that are all sort of more unique and opinionated?

John: Absolutely. I was five years incorrect on that one, but —

主持人: A hell of a prediction.

John: But — no, I mean, that's something I learned by working with non-computer-based designers and artists when I led RISD (Rhode Island School of Design). That was after a career at MIT that was all engineering, and then back to the handmade. And I was struck by how much human emotion, human pride lives in that community, and I felt it would have to surface through in the software industry.

But as a long-time proponent of computer programming for artisan designers — whether through Processing or Scratch — it was too hard for those people to engage. But now that floor of getting in has been lowered. Everyone's coming in. It's a really great time right now. It's also very confusing. So that's why Paul's approach is unique, in that it's high-quality and high-thought-based, which gives hope for more people to not choose the wrong solution.

十一、Impeccable 作为"设计思维的教学工具"

主持人: Paul, do you think there's an opportunity to start to, you know, shape the design mind through something like Impeccable? Because it feels like it touches on a part of the solution that no other design tool does.

Paul: That's a great question. I think for sure it can. And I think it can shape — ironically or interestingly — both the design and the engineering mind.

So I've had people walk up to me and say, "Well, we're now using Impeccable as a default thing that we installed on all of our engineers' and designers' computers." Why? Because they now communicate much better after using it. And I thought that was really interesting — like engineers are using design language more, and designers use engineering language more, because the tools, by using this careful vocabulary and approach, actually teach you what works, what doesn't, give you the language to express what you want and what you don't want.

And so I do think there's a teaching moment here. And I learned something by working with models all the time. I've always prompted that way, to be honest. I always wanted to get more out of models by having it test me and challenge me, and sort of like teach me new concepts. And there's some cool skills out there — like Matt's "Grill with Doc" skill, for instance. But Impeccable does that in some ways too. So I do think there's a lot of opportunity there, and that excites me.

十二、认知投降 vs. 认知委派

主持人: I think that really touches on a topic that is dear to my heart, and was part of a lot of discussions that I was in recently — and that's cognitive surrender versus cognitive delegation.

I don't know if you've heard those terms, but really, cognitive delegation is, you know, I'm using Google Maps, right? And Google Maps tells me where I can go as quickly as possible, right? I mean, I'm going to route my way to something.

But what if I let Google Maps decide where I want to go to? Now I kind of cognitively surrender to the application.

Paul: I think that's very true for LLMs if you're not careful. Like with LLMs — I mean, you, for instance, plan mode, right? It sounds like a good idea. You prompt something and then it creates a beautiful plan, and then it's eight pages long. You're not going to read through that plan, right? I mean, you're going to scroll through that plan and skim it, like, "I guess the model knows what it's doing." Right? And then you just click "okay."

And so now you kind of like surrendered yourself to the process. And there was a great conversation with a professor from Harvard B-School and a bunch of others, in a recent conference that I was at, where they touched on, you know, how students are now working differently, and how that is becoming a problem.

So anyway — I think cognitive delegation is great. I think delegating to the model is great. But preserving that point of view, making sure that you're still the one driving, is also really important. And I want, you working with Impeccable, to be the sort of game that back-and-forth where you're both active collaborators.

十三、模型有"品味"吗？

主持人: So, Paul — question for you on taste. Right now, automating things away, goals and loops, multi-agent orchestration — that's what everybody is talking about. But the question that nobody seems to be asking is: where does it all lead? Are we sort of, you know, as you said, surrendering human intent? And what is the role of human intent and taste, more broadly, in these products we're creating?

Paul: Yeah, I think it's an interesting question, because right now everybody's trying to solve or figure out whether models — whether LLMs have taste — right? As well as diffusion models.

And I think the answer is a little bit more complicated than saying yes or no. I think these models have millions of definitions of taste. They are trained on so many different interpretations of taste.

关键洞见：LLM 训练于"输出"，而非"输入"

But one thing that is very useful to know and think about is that LLMs have been trained on the output of humanity, not on the input. So what led to a design decision is not something that the LLMs know. So it's an approximation of taste, I would say, for a certain type of audience at a certain time.

So you can get pretty far if you know what you're asking for. But then the question is, whose taste is it?

Oftentimes taste is rare and human. And, you know, I think the way we talk about taste when we talk to humans — it's not something that can be easily recreated or spread to lots of different instances. And so I think — can the models be tasteful? Yeah, they can design tasteful things. But I also still think that something that has a human viewpoint goes far beyond.

And so I think amplifying human taste is an interesting problem — a more interesting problem than creating a replication of taste at the model layer.

主持人: Yeah, it's really interesting. If you sort of substitute the word "taste" for "humanity," it sort of carries the same meaning, perhaps even in a more powerful way.

十四、品味是天生的还是培养的？

主持人: John, do you think taste is innate, or is it something that's cultivated?

John: I think taste is always cultural. And different cultures have quote-unquote "higher taste" specifically because they've usually been around longer. They've had the material around longer, and therefore they've had maturity.

For instance, if you go to Denmark and you sit in any type of furniture, like a chair or sofa or table, you're like, "Wow, this is nice stuff." We walk around the US — it's like, I mean, I'm sorry, but not that great sometimes — because the US, we invented the styrofoam plate, for instance. You go to Japan: centuries of evolved approaches to raw materials. And not only that, scarcity of material. When raw material is scarce, we tend to make it more precious and design things well. Taste emerges through scarcity.

品味的皇室起源 vs. 材料的民主化

The last point on this is: we have to remember that design in the European sense came from royalty and the desire to be distinctive. "I've got fur. I've got gold. I've got emerald. I'm important. You're less important." Because they were working with scarce materials.

I think what's interesting about this era is that this idea of taste doesn't fit when all the materials are available to everyone.

主持人: Yeah, that's a really great point. That's absolutely fascinating.

十五、品味是耳语，速度是扩音器

主持人: Here's a comment from your recent blog, John, that I think I'll read:

The hard truth is that in rooms where decisions actually happen, taste is a whisper and velocity is a megaphone. It's almost impossible to defend a design nuance or instinct against a looming deadline. Engineering isn't the villain here. Incentives are. We build organizations that reward the measurable over the meaningful.

Agree? Disagree? How do you see that show up in the rooms that you're in?

John: I mean, it's all about the leader, right? We mentioned Steve Jobs — a unique combination of design, business, technical sense — that's very rare.

And I want to go back to Paul's stuff, and not to embarrass him again. So what's interesting about Impeccable is the design is very interesting from the API perspective. And Bill Atkinson, the engineer who recently passed away, who worked on the Macintosh — specifically, people may know this thing called QuickDraw, which was Apple's graphics library. Photoshop happened because of the design of QuickDraw's API.

It was impossible for there to be a Windows version of Photoshop because it didn't have the same API shape in DirectX. So I would argue that, Paul, design engineers are able to design the shape of the APIs really well. So they can work with agents as well as they can with humans. It's completely fascinating.

主持人: I had no idea. Like, what was the sort of unlock in the API surface that made Photoshop possible?

John: Oh my gosh. So the Macintosh's QuickDraw had this routine built upon this idea of regions. Everyone had polygons. The Mac had regions based on pixel shapes, which you could do logical set "or" — so things like flood fill were super-duper optimized for speed. So again, great APIs are not just shape, but performance.

主持人: Super interesting. That's absolutely fascinating.

十六、如何把直觉卖给一个看截止日期的领导

主持人: Paul, how do you think — maybe advice for our design engineers in the room — how have you in the past effectively communicated the value of an instinct versus, you know, a deadline or even another instinct which may be less informed?

Paul: Yeah, that's a tough one, because certainly I've failed many, many times on this one. And I've developed some strategies, but oftentimes it's about getting a leader into the right mindset to make a decision that I would make, right?

What I mean by that is — and John is right — I mean, if you have a lead who's mostly worked, who's mostly focused on economic outcomes, for example, during that week, right? Or mostly focused on performance. Ultimately, I think it's your job to present to them in a way that makes them dream the way you're dreaming about the future.

And that's something that I learned the hard way, over and over and over. You have to bring them along to where you want to go, and kind of get them on the same emotional journey. Maybe that's the right way to frame it.

And I think once they see what you see, once they see the type of future that you want to create — then you're on the same page. Now, that's way easier said than done. It's, I think, an art form to start with something that they need and then subtly shift in that conversation towards something that you both might want in the future.

I know that's maybe a bit abstract, but —

Conviction：奔向全局最大值的赌注

John: So on that topic, I had — I was having dinner like last week — you know, Jay Parikh is my boss, formerly at Meta. He's used this word that I hadn't heard in a while, which is conviction. And I think it's such a great word, because someone, a leader with quote-unquote "taste," has conviction. But it's not just like, you know, design-y conviction. It's not just business-y conviction. It's not just engineering conviction. It's that combination in a leader when they have conviction.

It is a bet — but it's a bet aimed at the global maximum, not the local maximum. And so I would say leaders that are doing that global-maximum conviction make a huge difference. And that's why product leaders — again, to embarrass Paul — I think Paul shot for a global maximum for design automation, and he hasn't given up yet. And that's a beautiful thing.

主持人: I love it. You know, Ben always says that the job of the CEO, the job of the leader, is not to have the idea, but to recognize the idea when they see it and bet the team on it.

Steve Jobs 的"测试"：双向的信念

Paul: Yeah. There's also another Steve Jobs sort of anecdote, right? Where he's very well known — or he was very well known — for shutting down people just to test them. Right? So the conviction is both on the leader and on the employee, interestingly.

Because very often somebody would walk into the room with Steve, and Steve would just test him and say, like, "you know, I think that's a stupid idea," even though he might think that's a really great idea. But just to test them, right? And to see, like, "no — well, will they just cave and walk out of the room? Or will they double down with 'no, no, no, Steve, I think you're wrong,' right?" And once he got that signal, once he got that signal of conviction, he often would say, "yes, let's do it."

And I think that's fascinating. So I think what John said is really interesting. I think it goes both ways.

John: Oh my gosh, that's a perfect example of LLM as judge. We have to, on both sides — on the generation of conviction and the feedback of conviction. You need both, the antimatter, matter. Love that.

主持人: I love that. Well, you know, we'll record this pod again in a year, and we'll see if all of the software that's been created through the new Git product, with assistance by Impeccable, turns out to be more thoughtful — and, you know, in that upper 10%. And that would be a fun outcome to see.

John: Accept it.

收尾：新工艺时代的展望

主持人: Incredible. John, any closing thoughts for us?

John: After picking up GitHub Design, I realized how so many people grew up at GitHub Design who were computational designers. There's Max at Notion who used to lead design at GitHub. There's all the Vercel people. They're all over the place. And I was asked to somehow bring back the franchise. And so I'm excited because Paul's work represents that kind of seminal, design-history-infused approach that can really make it easier for more engineers to design reliably, and also more designers to be able to reach for the skies.

主持人: I love it. Paul, any closing words?

Paul: Well, I'm just super, super excited to see what will happen once we've raised the floor, and what insane, crazy experiences we'll build afterwards and design afterwards. That's the kind of future that I want — as opposed to, you know, the bleak future where everything looks the same and people feel like they've surrendered themselves. And so I really am excited about that new era of craft. And that's something I'm working towards.

主持人: Extraordinary. Well, cheers to a new era of craft, thanks to the good work from both of you. Thank you, guys, both for being here. Excited to see the product out in the wild and play with it ourselves.

Paul & John: Thanks so much for having us.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：设计师比工程师更会"驯服"AI
视频中最锋利的一刀来自 Paul：**同一个 Claude、同一个网站、同一套设计系统，设计师得到的输出始终优于工程师**。差别不在模型，而在词汇——"vertical rhythm"、"negative space"、"quieter"、"bolder"，这些是工程师从不使用的"咒语"。
这意味着一个被严重低估的事实：**Prompt 工程的瓶颈不是逻辑能力，而是领域词汇的密度**。LLM 时代真正的"特权阶层"不是会写代码的人，而是手里握着精确隐喻系统的人——诗人、爵士乐手、香水调配师、武术教练。他们的"行业黑话"在前 AI 时代是壁垒，在后 AI 时代是杠杆。Impeccable 本质上是把设计师的"黑话词典"塞进了 agent harness，等于给所有人发了一本"魔法咒语手册"。
## 二、跨领域连接：紫色渐变 → Tailwind 默认值 → 路易十四的紫袍
Paul 指出 AI 生成的 UI 普遍是"紫色渐变"，**根源是 Tailwind 的默认主题里有紫色**。这是一个微观的"默认值暴政"案例。但 Jo

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] When Rome Rioted for More Nepotism – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：暴民其实在为"裙带关系"游行

我们习惯把"反腐败"想象成永恒的民意——但 1500 年代罗马街头的暴动方向恰恰相反：人民要求教皇任用他的私生子，而不是那位更有能力的将军。

这里藏着一个被现代人系统性误读的真相：在一个没有"制度性信任"的社会里，裙带关系不是腐败，而是基础设施。它扮演的角色等同于今天的征信系统、合同法、独立司法和宪政忠诚加在一起。私生子之所以"可信"，不是因为他贤能，而是因为他的命运与教皇绑死——他无法背叛，因为他没有第二条生路。

把这一点抽象出来就是：当社会缺乏抽象忠诚的载体（宪法、国家、品牌、协议）时，生物性绑定（血缘、师承、姻亲）就是最低成本的信任协议。这解释了为什么今天东亚家族企业、华尔街校友网、硅谷 YC 同期、甚至加密世界的"巨鲸朋友圈"在制度真空处反复重演这一模式——裙带不是文明的失败，而是文明在低带宽通信下的默认解。

二、一个让你脊背发凉的跨领域连接：中世纪审判 ≈ 现代的"算法量刑 + 关系赦免"

Palmer 描述的中世纪司法系统看似荒谬——法条上写着"偷东西判死"，实际 100 个案子里只有 1 个真死。我们直觉认为这是"执法不严"。但她揭示出真正的结构：

重刑是默认值，赦免才是产品。

这与今天两个截然不同领域有惊人同构：

  • 现代美国刑事司法：检察官以最重指控起诉（"throw the book"），90% 以上案件靠 plea bargain（认罪协商）解决。表面温和的量刑背后，是一个靠"威胁性默认值 + 关系性减免"运作的系统。你的律师、你的"关系"、你能否表现出悔意，决定了你掉进哪一档。
  • 算法时代的内容审核：平台规则写着"违规即封号"，但实际处理因 KOL 等级、广告主关系、申诉渠道而剧烈分化。Twitter 蓝标、YouTube partner manager、微信"白名单"——就是现代版的 patron。

更深一层：人类社会似乎反复回到"严苛默认 + 关系性赦免"这种结构，因为它同时满足两个矛盾需求——震慑（靠重刑）和弹性（靠人情）。启蒙运动的"比例正义"反而是一个历史异类，它要求每个个案都达到统计公平，代价是巨大的制度成本（独立法官、律师、上诉、陪审团……）。我们可能正在悄悄退回前启蒙模式而不自知。

三、最深的系统性洞见：审判的"神学 UX"

最让我震撼的是 Palmer 揭示的中世纪审判的真实产品目标：它根本不是为了"做出正确判决"——

它是一个"末日审判的预演 VR 体验"。

被告恐惧、绝望、向 patron 哀求、最终获得赦免——整个流程被设计成模拟灵魂在神面前的体验，目的是"内在矫正"。即使被处死的人，也由教士握着手、举着圣母像走向刑场，确保最后一刻想着天堂。

这意味着：中世纪人对"司法系统"的概念，更接近我们今天对"沉浸式戏剧"或"宗教仪式"的概念，而不是对"法律"的概念。他们和我们用同一个词（justice），但指向的根本不是同一种东西。

由此引出一个让人不安的元问题：

今天我们叫"教育"、"医疗"、"司法"、"民主"的这些系统，五百年后的人回看时，会不会发现它们的真实功能也完全不是字面意思？

比如——现代大学的真实功能可能不是"传授知识"，而是"四年的身份重塑仪式 + 婚配市场 + 阶层筛选器"。我们用"教育"这个词包装它，就像中世纪人用"justice"包装一场灵魂剧场。Palmer 这段话最锋利的地方在于——她不是在讲历史，她在示范一种"系统考古学"的方法：把任何一个社会的核心词汇剥开，去看它真正在做什么。

📝 转录文本

一、问题缘起：500 年前与今天的根本差异

One big way in which our world is different from 500 years ago is this — the focus on patronage and that being the basis of political power. It was much more prominent, right? So that is something I think would be interesting to understand.

Palmer： Well, it's not that it was just more prominent, but it was the fundamental glue of the society as opposed to one of several glues of the society. And you know, patronage — which was also familial and therefore entangled with nepotism — was so fundamental that, you know, for example, when Alessandro Farnese was elected Pope Paul III in the middle of the 1500s, he didn't corruptly make one of his kinsmen commander of the papal armies. He instead appointed a really competent, experienced general instead of his own not very competent illegitimate son.

二、罗马为"更多裙带关系"而暴动

And there were riots in Rome. "Your Holiness, the people demand more nepotism. You must appoint your illegitimate son to command your armies, because your illegitimate son will never betray you. And we will know we can trust the papal armies not to turn on Rome if the Pope's son is the commander. And we don't know that about this other commander. He might turn against Your Holiness, and there might be a rift between the Pope and the papal armies if he's not somebody that rises and falls with you the way your son does."

Therefore, by popular demand, the people want more nepotism — because the system depends on it. Right. And that's how you see how the system depends on there being levels of trust that the patronage system creates, because it involves multi-generational entanglement of families: if these families rise, they rise together; if they fall, they fall together. Which creates levels of trust that can sustain things like this world where the oath of a soldier is to his commander, not to the polity that he serves.

And in modernity, we realize another solution to that is: the oath of the soldier is not to the commander; the oath of the soldier is to the constitution, or to the country, or to the people. But in this period, the oath of the soldier is to the commander, mostly because communications are so slow that the commander has to be able to give speedy field commands. But it means you're creating an army and you're handing it to a man. And if you cannot trust that man, then the people will be terrified that there could be a rift between Rome and its own armies, or between Rome and its treasurer, or between Rome and its other allies.

Patronage is the glue that makes things work all the way down.

三、从审判记录看 patronage 如何运作

Palmer： All the way down to the level of: if you need a defense attorney, that's done through patronage. The outcomes of trials are a really great way to see patronage.

So, we're all familiar with the fact that law codes in the Middle Ages are really cruel, right? And it's like death for everything. Death for theft, death for adultery, death for homosexuality, death for setting fire to the prince's beehive — whatever it is, you know, that's the sentence on the books. And then you look at the actual trial records, and maybe one in a hundred convictions for that crime actually ends in a capital sentence. And almost all of the other ones end in a fine or a public flogging, but not in the sentence that's on the books.

And we say, "Why? And how is that happening?" Patronage is the answer.

案例：木匠儿子的谋杀指控

So, if it's the Middle Ages or the Renaissance, and you're a carpenter, and your teenage son gets drunk and he punches somebody in a brawl and breaks the guy's nose in that way that makes him die — accidentally kills the guy in a drunken brawl — and your son is now on trial for murder.

You're a carpenter. You therefore have worked for the rich family whose family carpenter you are, right? Let's say it's the Medici family. Whenever they need new pews for the family church, or new furniture, or repairs for the family gates, they go to you. So, you go to them and you say, "My son is in danger. He's on trial. Please put in a good word." And your patron has the ability to then influence the judges. And they will put in a good word for you, and then you will get a lighter sentence.

This is sort of an ancestor of what having a character witness is — to say "but so-and-so is such a good person, they should have the milder punishment, not the more severe one." But the norm is: you're accused of a severe crime, you're put on trial for your life, your patron intervenes, and you get a lighter sentence. And this is how justice is supposed to work.

四、启蒙之前的"司法"：灵魂矫正而非比例正义

And this is a very severe line that changes in the 18th century with the Enlightenment. Because we now think of proportional justice: the sentence for the crime should be this. And ideal justice is everyone who is guilty of the crime gets that sentence. That is fair. It doesn't matter whether you're rich or poor, the sentence should be the same. This is the ideal of Enlightenment justice.

The ideal of this period's justice, which is much more shaped by Christianity, is: the purpose of the trial is the spiritual interior correction of the soul of the sinner. And therefore the ideal outcome is for them to fear for their life. They're before a terrifying judge who is the earthly representation of God. And they're before him, they know that they're guilty and they deserve to be thrown into the pits of hell. But miraculously, they are given grace and they are pardoned.

And the process of being put on trial, fearing for your life, begging to the patron, and then receiving mercy is supposed to sort of be an earthly preview of the process your soul will undergo when you are before divine judgment. And therefore should make you come out the other end a good person. And the goal of the justice system is the spiritual improvement of the sinner, in the hope that they will come out the other end better and more likely to go to heaven.

Even when the people are being sentenced to death, there are religious organizations who sit with them overnight, having a final prayer group, and walk with them to the gallows holding their hand — and holding a painting of the Virgin Mary in front of their face, so that to the very last moment the person who's about to be executed is thinking about heaven. And the ideal outcome of the execution is that the soul goes to heaven.

So the whole structure of the justice system expects the intervention of a patron, who represents the intervention of a patron saint, persuading the judge — who is God — to give you mercy.

五、那 1% 被处决的人：失去了 patron 的人

So when we see a hundred trials end in 99 the person paid a small fine, and one the person was executed — what that actually means is: in 99, their patron stepped in. Somebody persuaded somebody, who put in a good word, who got the light sentence. And in one, that person had fallen out of the patronage network. That person had angered their boss, their protector. That's why it went all the way to being a capital offense.

案例：乔尔丹诺·布鲁诺

Probably a lot of people listening are familiar with Giordano Bruno, very famous as a martyr for science because he was burnt at the stake by the Inquisition. Fewer people know that that was not his first Inquisition trial. He was investigated a number of times by the Inquisition for doing various radical forms of thought. The earlier trials had the usual outcome for that kind of trial of: he had a patron, there were rich people that he worked with or for, the university was hosting him, they put in a good word, he's fine. The Inquisition tells him "be good," and things continue as they are.

That time, he had angered the person he worked for. He pissed off his patron. And it's his patron who turns him into the Inquisition and says, "This guy is a charlatan. He promised he could teach me these things that he can't. I don't trust him. He's no good. Throw the book at him." And the reason that trial goes all the way to a capital sentence is that he doesn't have a patron. He's the 99th case that time. And if he had had a patron protecting him, despite how radical his stuff was, he would have been okay.

反向案例：皮科·德拉·米兰多拉

And we see that in the trial of Giovanni Pico della Mirandola, who was candidly substantially more radical than Giordano Bruno. But when Pico is on trial, Lorenzo de' Medici and other powerful people really care about Pico, and they pull out all the stops, and, you know, Lorenzo talks to his brother-in-law who's an Orsini. The Orsini have enormous influence in Rome. They get permission for Pico to be let go and sent home to Lorenzo, to sort of live under house arrest under Lorenzo's promise that he'll be good from then on.

反向案例：费奇诺与"史上最强推荐信"

Or Marsilio Ficino, right, who is this radical Platonist who publishes a book on how to project your soul outside of time and summon angels, and arguing for the existence of reincarnation, and is very clearly being extremely theologically weird. This is the man who wrote the best letter of recommendation ever written in the history of time, when he was recommending a young scholar for a job with the King of Hungary. And he writes in the recommendation letter:

"This young man is the reincarnation of Saint Thomas Aquinas. So you should give him a job."

Now, that is a letter of recommendation. But you're like, "The reincarnation of Saint Thomas Aquinas, huh?" And the Inquisition comes knocking on Ficino's door and is like, "Mhm — reincarnation?" And Ficino is like, "Oh no. Help. Talk to Lorenzo."

Lorenzo talks to his brother, Cardinal Orsini. Cardinal Orsini shuts it down. And Ficino is told, "Mhm, maybe lay off talking quite so overtly about the reincarnation." And Ficino says, "Yes, of course. And I will only teach very pious people how to summon angels and project their souls out of their bodies. I promise I won't teach it to anybody who will use these powers irresponsibly." The Inquisition is like, "Okay." And goes home.

Because patronage kicked in.

六、结语：连买一只苹果都需要 patron

And patronage is the glue that makes everything work. 'Cause you can't even stay in a hotel or buy an apple — I'm not kidding — without a patron. Because, you know, you arrive at a city, nobody knows you, you're a stranger. What you have is a letter of recommendation from your patron, who's friends with some important person there. You present that at the hotel. That's why they let you stay.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：暴民其实在为"裙带关系"游行
我们习惯把"反腐败"想象成永恒的民意——但 1500 年代罗马街头的暴动方向恰恰相反：**人民要求教皇任用他的私生子，而不是那位更有能力的将军**。
这里藏着一个被现代人系统性误读的真相：**在一个没有"制度性信任"的社会里，裙带关系不是腐败，而是基础设施**。它扮演的角色等同于今天的征信系统、合同法、独立司法和宪政忠诚加在一起。私生子之所以"可信"，不是因为他贤能，而是因为他的命运与教皇**绑死**——他无法背叛，因为他没有第二条生路。
把这一点抽象出来就是：**当社会缺乏抽象忠诚的载体（宪法、国家、品牌、协议）时，生物性绑定（血缘、师承、姻亲）就是最低成本的信任协议**。这解释了为什么今天东亚家族企业、华尔街校友网、硅谷 YC 同期、甚至加密世界的"巨鲸朋友圈"在制度真空处反复重演这一模式——**裙带不是文明的失败，而是文明在低带宽通信下的默认解**。
## 二、一个让你脊背发凉的跨领域连接：中世纪审判 ≈ 现代的"算法量刑 + 关系赦免"
Palmer 描述的中世纪司法系统看似荒谬——法条上写着"偷东西判死"，实际

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Is the Bitcoin Treasury Thesis Finally Breaking?
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"木头电脑"作为一种隐喻：比特币财库论的真正裂痕

视频标题问"比特币财库论是否终于在破裂"，而你给我的转录内容却是一段关于木质电脑的闲聊——这本身就是最具讽刺意味的洞察。比特币财库公司（MicroStrategy 模式）本质上就是一台"木头电脑"：把一种古老的、被认为"温暖、有质感、稀缺"的材料（BTC），强行嵌入到一个需要高速散热、流动性和资本周转的现代金融机器外壳里。

人们问的不是"它能不能跑"，而是"它会不会烧起来"。答案永远是同一句话："只要处理得当，并保持良好的气流，就没问题。" ——翻译成金融语言就是：只要溢价（mNAV）不崩、再融资窗口不关、市场情绪不熄火，它就能继续运转。三个条件里任何一个失效，木头就开始冒烟。

二、出人意料的观察：财库论的真正敌人不是熊市，而是"被模仿"

大多数分析者盯着 BTC 价格、利率、可转债到期日。但真正在杀死财库论的，是模仿者本身。当只有 MicroStrategy 一家做这件事时，它是"创新套利"；当 200 家上市公司都在做同一件事时，它变成了"拥挤交易"——而拥挤交易的特征是：溢价坍塌的速度远快于资产价格的下跌。

这就回到木头电脑的比喻：第一台木头电脑是艺术品，第一百台木头电脑是库存。稀缺性的载体一旦被复制，载体本身就不再稀缺，哪怕它装的还是同样稀缺的 BTC。

三、跨领域连接：江户时代的"米券"、17 世纪的郁金香期货、与今天的 BTC 财库股

历史上有三次"用证券化包装稀缺实物"的实验： - 大阪堂岛米市场（1730）：用米券交易未来的大米，最终米券的价格波动远大于大米本身。 - 荷兰郁金香期货（1636）：合约脱离球茎独立运行，崩盘时合约先死，球茎还在。 - 黄金 ETF（2004 至今）：相对成功，因为它的溢价被套利机制锁死在接近 1。

比特币财库股的结构最像堂岛米券，而不是黄金 ETF——因为它没有内置的赎回/套利通道把 mNAV 拉回 1。这意味着它的命运在结构上已经被写好：溢价可以飞向月球，也可以瞬间归零，但它无法稳定停留在中间。

所以视频的真正问题或许应该重写为："我们是不是终于意识到，我们买的不是比特币，而是一张关于比特币的、可能着火的木头收据？"

提示：你贴出的转录内容只有四句关于木质电脑的对话，与视频标题完全不匹配。下方 <formatted> 我只能基于你实际提供的文本进行分段美化，无法凭空补全播客内容。如需对完整播客做分析，请贴出完整转录。

📝 转录文本

开场：一台不寻常的电脑

A： Hey, check this out. It's a wooden computer.

B： Wait, is that even safe? Doesn't wood catch fire?

解释：安全性的关键条件

A： If it's treated properly and has good airflow, it's fine.

结尾：对质感的赞叹

B： That looks amazing — the texture is so unique.

观看原视频 →
